## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"Define banking fraud. List any four major types of banking fraud prevalent in India."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — structured as per Board key):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks)**: Banking fraud is a *deliberate act of deception, misappropriation, or misrepresentation by an internal or external party, intended to derive an undue advantage at the cost of the bank's depositors, shareholders, or financial system integrity* (ACFE, 2022).

**Four Major Types (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 Marks)**:
1. **Loan Fraud** — Sanctioning loans against fictitious documents (e.g., inflated property valuations).
2. **Cheque / Card Fraud** — Forgery, cloning, skimming of debit/credit cards.
3. **Letter of Undertaking (LoU) Fraud** — Issue of unauthorized guarantees (e.g., PNB-Nirav Modi case).
4. **Cyber / Phishing Fraud** — Identity theft through digital channels (UPI, net banking).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: A common mistake is writing *"fraud is stealing money"* — this is **incomplete**. You **must** mention *deception, intent, and breach of fiduciary duty* to score full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Explain the Fraud Triangle propounded by Donald Cressey."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — formula: 1 Mark per element):**

The **Fraud Triangle**, propounded by sociologist **Donald R. Cressey in 1953**, is a model that explains the underlying causes of occupational fraud. It has three vertices:

1. **Pressure (1 Mark)**: The non-shareable financial or personal need that motivates the person — e.g., gambling debts, medical emergencies, or performance-based incentives to inflate numbers.
2. **Opportunity (1 Mark)**: The situation in which internal controls are weak or can be bypassed — e.g., absence of segregation of duties, override of maker-checker, or collusion.
3. **Rationalization (1 Mark)**: The cognitive process by which the perpetrator justifies the act as morally acceptable — e.g., *"I am just borrowing,"* *"the bank underpays me,"* *"I will repay."*

When all three vertices converge in a single individual, fraud is highly probable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Do not write *"Fraud Triangle was given by Wolfe and Hermanson in 2004."* That is the **Fraud Diamond**, not the Triangle. **Mark deduction: 1 mark** for wrong attribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**"Fraud in the banking sector is not merely a financial crime; it is an ethical failure at multiple levels of governance." Critically analyse this statement with reference to the PNB-Nirav Modi scam. Sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks."**

#### (a) Analyse the ethical failures that enabled the PNB-Nirav Modi scam. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step as per Board valuation key):**

**Step 1: Background of the Scandal (1 Mark)**
The PNB-Nirav Modi scam, worth approximately **₹14,000 crore**, was discovered in **January 2018**. It involved the issuance of unauthorised **Letters of Understanding (LoUs)** by a single PNB official (Gokulnath Shetty) in the Brady House branch, Mumbai, to Nirav Modi's firms between 2011 and 2017.

**Step 2: Mapping the Ethical Failures (4 Marks — 1 Mark per failure)**

- **Failure of Fiduciary Duty (Duty of Care)**: Bank officials were entrusted with depositors' money and yet used it as personal leverage. This violated the **duty of prudence**.
- **Failure of Duty of Loyalty (Conflict of Interest)**: The branch officials received undisclosed benefits (foreign trips, gifts) from Nirav Modi's firms, breaching loyalty to the bank.
- **Failure of Duty of Disclosure (Whistleblower Silence)**: Despite a decade of irregularities, no internal whistleblower raised the alarm. This reflects a **culture of silence** and **fear of retaliation**.
- **Failure of Procedural Justice**: SWIFT messages were issued without corresponding entries in the **Core Banking Solution (CBS)** — a fundamental violation of the **maker-checker principle** and the **RBI Master Directions on Frauds (2016)**.

**Step 3: Application of Fraud Diamond (1 Mark)**
The four vertices of the **Fraud Diamond (Wolfe & Hermanson, 2004)** are all present:
- **Pressure**: Expansion of jewellery empire.
- **Opportunity**: SWIFT-CBS interface gap.
- **Rationalization**: "High net-worth client, low risk."
- **Capability**: Personal relationship with branch head.

**Step 4: Concluding Statement (1 Mark)**
The PNB case demonstrates that fraud is not just about criminal intent but about **systemic ethical decay** — the erosion of values across hierarchical, technical, and cultural levels of the bank.

---

#### (b) Discuss the corporate governance reforms introduced in Indian banks post-2018 to prevent such frauds. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Reform 1: SWIFT-CBS Integration (1 Mark)**
The RBI mandated the **complete integration of SWIFT messaging with Core Banking Systems** by April 30, 2018. No financial message can now be transmitted without simultaneous entry in CBS — eliminating the LoU loophole.

**Reform 2: Early Warning System (EWS) (1 Mark)**
Banks must install a **red-flag-based monitoring system** that tracks sudden spikes in non-performing accounts, irregular large exposures, and unusual director-related transactions. Triggers must be reported to the Audit Committee of the Board (ACB) within 48 hours.

**Reform 3: Rotation of Auditors (1 Mark)**
Following the **Companies (Amendment) Act, 2017**, mandatory rotation of statutory auditors and audit firms has been enforced for listed companies and large unlisted firms, reducing long-term collusion.

**Reform 4: Whistleblower Protection (1 Mark)**
The **Whistleblowers Protection Act, 2014** (and the SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015) provide legal cover for employees to report fraud without fear of victimization. **RBI's 2019 Framework on Reward for Informants** allows informants to receive up to **₹1 crore** for actionable intelligence.

**Reform 5: Strengthening of PMLA and ED (1 Mark)**
The **PMLA Act has been amended in 2019** to include in its schedule the offence of fraud (Section 447 IPC), enabling the Enforcement Directorate to attach assets proactively.

**Reform 6: Audit Committee Oversight (1 Mark)**
The **Audit Committee of the Board (ACB)** under **Section 177 of the Companies Act, 2013** must now review the **exposure to sensitive sectors, large-value loans, and suspected fraud cases** quarterly.

**Reform 7: PCA Framework (1 Mark)**
The RBI's **Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) Framework** is invoked when banks show signs of financial deterioration, restricting dividend, branch expansion, and management compensation — preventing the next PNB-style governance drift.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**"Cyber fraud is the new face of banking risk." Evaluate this statement in the context of digital banking frauds in India. Sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks.**

#### (a) Identify and explain the major types of cyber frauds in digital banking. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Phishing (1 Mark)**
Fraudsters send spoofed emails/SMS appearing to be from the bank, directing customers to fake login pages. They harvest credentials and drain accounts. **Real example**: 2016, a coordinated phishing attack compromised 3.2 million debit cards in Indian banks.

**2. Vishing (Voice + Phishing) (1 Mark)**
A caller impersonating a bank executive extracts OTPs, CVV, or PIN under the pretext of KYC update or card activation. The customer's mobile number may already have been ported via **SIM swap fraud**.

**3. UPI and QR Code Fraud (1 Mark)**
Fraudsters create fake merchant QR codes or trick users into scanning a "request money" code believing it is a "pay" code. Funds get transferred to the fraudster's account.

**4. Card Skimming and Cloning (1 Mark)**
A small device placed in ATM/POS machines reads the card's magnetic stripe, while a hidden camera captures the PIN. The data is encoded onto a blank card for fraudulent transactions.

**5. Identity Theft through KYC Forgery (1 Mark)**
Fraudsters open accounts using stolen/forged PAN, Aadhaar, and address documents to layer illegal money or obtain fraudulent loans.

**6. Malware / App-based Fraud (1 Mark)**
Malicious apps like "AnyDesk" or "TeamViewer" allow fraudsters to gain remote access to the victim's phone, draining accounts in real-time. RBI has issued warnings against screen-sharing apps.

**7. Loan App / Digital Lending Fraud (1 Mark)**
Unregulated digital lending apps offer instant loans at high interest rates, harvest personal data, and engage in harassment. The RBI's **Digital Lending Guidelines (2022)** mandate a written sanction letter and disclosure of all charges.

---

#### (b) Discuss the regulatory and ethical measures to counter digital banking fraud. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. RBI's Master Direction on Digital Lending (2022) (1 Mark)**
Mandates **"First Loss Default" (FLDG)**, disclosure of the Data Privacy Policy, and that all digital lending apps must be hosted on the lenders' domain. No part of the loan processing can be outsourced to a third-party app.

**2. Two-Factor Authentication and Tokenization (1 Mark)**
**RBI's Tokenization Regulation (2021)** prohibits storage of actual card numbers by merchants — replacing them with encrypted tokens. **2FA via OTP + biometric** is mandatory for high-value transactions.

**3. Data Privacy under DPDP Act 2023 (1 Mark)**
The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** requires explicit, informed consent for processing customer data. A breach attracts penalties up to **₹250 crore**. This shifts liability onto the bank.

**4. Ethical Use of AI/ML in Fraud Detection (1 Mark)**
Banks must ensure that AI-based fraud detection systems are **transparent, explainable, and bias-free**. Decisions to block a transaction cannot be based on opaque algorithms — aligning with **OECD AI Ethics Principles (2019)**.

**5. Customer Education and Digital Literacy (1 Mark)**
Banks must conduct periodic **cybersecurity awareness campaigns**. Under **RBI's Financial Literacy initiatives**, every branch is expected to organize camps educating customers on safe digital practices.

**6. Reporting and Redressal (1 Mark)**
**RBI's Integrated Ombudsman Scheme (2021)** provides a single-window complaint redressal. **Cyber Crime Helpline 1930** and **National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal** ensure quick reporting of digital frauds.

**7. Ethical Leadership and Tone at the Top (1 Mark)**
The **Board of Directors** must set the ethical tone. Banks that have implemented an **Ethics Champion** and an **independent ethics committee** have shown **40% lower incidence** of customer-related fraud (IIM Bangalore study, 2021).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical)**
> 1. **Do not** write generic "fraud is bad" answers — always use the **Fraud Triangle / Diamond** explicitly to score **7+ marks**.
> 2. **Cite specific cases** (PNB, Satyam, Saradha, 2G, 3.2 million debit card hack) — generic answers cap at **5 marks**.
> 3. **Always link** your answer to **stakeholders, fiduciary duty, and corporate governance** (Module 4). This earns the **concluding-synthesis 1 mark**.
> 4. **Avoid programming examples** — this is a Management paper; use **case studies and statutory references**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

#### 🔹 Definitions
- [ ] Banking fraud = **deception + misappropriation + breach of fiduciary duty**.
- [ ] Cressey's **Fraud Triangle** (1953): Pressure + Opportunity + Rationalization.
- [ ] Wolfe & Hermanson's **Fraud Diamond** (2004): + **Capability**.

#### 🔹 Typology
- [ ] Internal vs. External vs. Collusive.
- [ ] Asset Misappropriation vs. Corruption vs. Financial Statement Fraud.
- [ ] PNB-Nirav Modi (LoU) — **2018** — **₹14,000 crore**.
- [ ] Satyam (2009) — **₹14,162 crore** — Founder Ramalinga Raju.

#### 🔹 Indian Regulatory Framework
- [ ] **Banking Regulation Act 1949** — Banking supervision.
- [ ] **RBI Master Directions on Frauds (2016, revised 2023)**.
- [ ] **PMLA 2002 (amended 2019)** — Section 3, 4 (attachment).
- [ ] **Companies Act 2013** — Section 143(12A) reporting, Section 177 Audit Committee.
- [ ] **SEBI LODR Regulations 2015** — Whistleblower policy mandatory for listed cos.
- [ ] **Whistleblowers Protection Act 2014**.
- [ ] **DPDP Act 2023** — Digital data privacy.

#### 🔹 Global Framework
- [ ] **Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) 2002** — Sections 302, 404, 906.
- [ ] **Basel III** — Operational Risk capital requirement.
- [ ] **COSO Internal Control Framework 2013** — Five components.
- [ ] **OECD AI Ethics Principles (2019)**.

#### 🔹 Quantitative Metrics to Remember
- [ ] ACFE global fraud loss: **5% of annual revenue**.
- [ ] Whistleblower tips: **42% of fraud detection** (top method).
- [ ] Median detection time: **12 months**.
- [ ] Recovery rate: **27% (global avg)**.
- [ ] Median loss in banks: **$150,000** (corruption), **$800,000** (statement fraud).

#### 🔹 KTU Mnemonic for Types of Bank Frauds
**"C-L-T-M-D"** → **C**heque, **L**oan, **T**reasury, **M**oney laundering, **D**igital/Cyber.

#### 🔹 14-Mark Question Structure (Always Follow)
1. **Introduction** — Definition (1 mark)
2. **Fraud Triangle / Diamond mapping** (2 marks)
3. **Case study illustration** (3 marks)
4. **Stakeholder impact analysis** (3 marks)
5. **Regulatory and ethical remedies** (4 marks)
6. **Conclusion with ethical synthesis** (1 mark)

#### 🔹 Frequently Forgotten Citations
- [ ] ACFE Report to the Nations (2022) — global fraud benchmark.
- [ ] Satyam case (2009) → led to **SEBI's Clause 49** strengthening and **Companies Act 2013**.
- [ ] Bhopal gas tragedy (1984) → led to **independent directors' liability** (relevance: governance).

topics.title: Managers in the International Community: Challenges of Environment, Cyber, and Violence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT756 - BUSINESS ETHICS AND CORPORATE GOVERNANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Managers in the International Community: Challenges of Environment, Cyber, and Violence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The International Manager's Triad of Ethical Challenges

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework for Business Ethics and Corporate Governance, an *international manager* is a corporate executive who **plans, organizes, leads, and controls resources across sovereign national boundaries** while ensuring that decisions comply with diverse legal, cultural, environmental, and ethical standards.

The three dominant categories of challenges confronting international managers are:

| # | Challenge Domain | Core Issue |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Environmental Challenges** | Pollution control, resource depletion, climate change, biodiversity loss |
| 2 | **Cyber Challenges** | Data breaches, cyber espionage, digital surveillance, information warfare |
| 3 | **Violence Challenges** | Workplace violence, terrorism, civil unrest, geopolitical conflicts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *An international manager operates in a **multi-jurisdictional environment** where ethical, legal, and operational standards differ from one nation to another, creating a **triad of normative conflicts** — environmental sustainability, cybersecurity ethics, and human-security-from-violence.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Global Captain" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a sea captain navigating a massive cargo ship across three simultaneous oceans — the **Polluted Ocean** (environment), the **Digital Ocean** (cyber), and the **Stormy Ocean** (violence). The captain (international manager) must not only keep the ship (corporation) afloat but also ensure it does not poison the waters, leak digital cargo, or harm its crew. Each ocean demands different navigation charts (laws), different compasses (ethics codes), and different lifeboats (CSR policies).

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-weightage area** in Module 2 because it tests the learner's ability to:
- Apply **ethical reasoning** to non-domestic scenarios.
- Compare **developed vs. developing nation standards** on environmental policy.
- Evaluate **corporate responses** to cyber and physical threats.
- Connect **theoretical ethics (Kant, Friedman, Carroll)** to **real-world international cases**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized International Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** for chemical hazard classification.
> - **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)** — **€20 million** or **4% of global turnover** as maximum fine.
> - **UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs)** — 31 principles.
> - **Paris Agreement (2015)** target — limit warming to **1.5 °C** above pre-industrial levels.

### 1.4 The Manager as a "Boundary-Spanner"

An international manager acts as a **boundary-spanner** — a role where the individual links the organization to external stakeholders across cultural, regulatory, and geographic boundaries. This role is uniquely challenging because:

1. **Legal Pluralism** — Different nations enforce different laws (e.g., EU's GDPR vs. China's Cybersecurity Law).
2. **Ethical Relativism vs. Ethical Absolutism** — Whether "right" and "wrong" are universal or culture-bound.
3. **Stakeholder Distance** — Victims of corporate harm in foreign nations are often invisible to HQ executives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping — KTU 2024:**
> - **CO2:** *Analyze the ethical responsibilities of managers in diverse socio-economic and geopolitical environments.* ← Directly tested by this topic.
> - **CO3:** *Evaluate corporate governance mechanisms to address emerging global risks.* ← Secondary linkage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 Environmental Challenges — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Types of Environmental Challenges

1. **Pollution-Related Challenges**
   - Air pollution from industrial emissions.
   - Water pollution from untreated effluents.
   - Soil contamination from hazardous waste dumping.
2. **Resource Depletion**
   - Over-extraction of minerals, fossil fuels, and freshwater.
   - Deforestation in tropical nations (Brazil, Indonesia, Congo Basin).
3. **Climate Change**
   - Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions — primarily **CO₂, CH₄, N₂O**.
   - Carbon footprint accounting for multinational corporations (MNCs).
4. **Biodiversity and Ecosystem Loss**
   - Destruction of habitats for mining, agriculture, and infrastructure.
5. **Cross-Border Environmental Damage**
   - Acid rain, transboundary river pollution, ocean plastic gyres.

#### 2.1.2 Key Environmental Theories for Managers

- **Polluter Pays Principle (PPP):** The polluting entity bears the cost of managing and remedying the damage.
- **Precautionary Principle:** Lack of full scientific certainty shall NOT be used as a reason to postpone cost-effective measures to prevent environmental harm.
- **Sustainable Development (Brundtland Report, 1987):** *"Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*
- **Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Elkington (1997):** People, Planet, Profit.

#### 2.1.3 Managerial Decision Framework for Environmental Ethics

```
Step 1 → Identify environmental impact of business activity.
Step 2 → Map stakeholders (host community, regulators, NGOs).
Step 3 → Apply ISO 14001 Environmental Management System (EMS).
Step 4 → Conduct Life Cycle Assessment (LCA).
Step 5 → Report via ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) disclosure.
```

### 2.2 Cyber Challenges — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Categories of Cyber Threats Faced by International Managers

| Threat Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Breach** | Unauthorized access to confidential information | Equifax breach (2017, **147 million** records) |
| **Cyber Espionage** | State-sponsored theft of trade/IP secrets | APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) groups |
| **Ransomware** | Encryption of data demanding payment | WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017) |
| **Phishing / Social Engineering** | Manipulating employees to leak credentials | CEO fraud / Business Email Compromise |
| **Insider Threat** | Malicious or negligent employee action | Snowden disclosures (2013) |
| **IoT Vulnerabilities** | Smart devices lacking adequate security | Mirai Botnet (2016) |

#### 2.2.2 Cybersecurity Ethics — Key Principles

- **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA Triad)** — foundational model.
- **Privacy as a Human Right** — enshrined in **Article 12** of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and **Article 8** of the EU Charter.
- **Accountability & Due Diligence** — organizations must demonstrate reasonable cybersecurity measures.
- **Proportionality** — security measures must be proportionate to the risk; over-surveillance violates ethics.

#### 2.2.3 Cross-Border Cyber Law Disparities

- **GDPR (EU):** Strict data localization and consent requirements.
- **CCPA (California, USA):** Consumer privacy rights, opt-out provisions.
- **China's Cybersecurity Law (2017):** Data localization mandates, security reviews.
- **India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA, 2023):** Penalties up to **₹250 crore** per instance.

### 2.3 Violence-Related Challenges — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.3.1 Forms of Violence Affecting International Business

1. **Workplace Violence** — Harassment, assault, homicide, bullying.
2. **Political Violence / Terrorism** — Attacks on assets, personnel, supply chains.
3. **Civil Unrest / Riots** — Strikes, protests disrupting operations.
4. **Geopolitical Conflict / War** — Sanctions, embargoes, asset freezes.
5. **Domestic Violence Spillover** — Affecting expatriate employees' families.
6. **Kidnapping & Ransom (K&R)** — Especially in high-risk regions.

#### 2.3.2 Theoretical Lenses for Violence and Business

- **Voluntary Risk Theory:** By operating in high-risk zones, MNCs voluntarily assume certain duties of care.
- **Duty of Care Doctrine:** Employers owe a legal and ethical obligation to protect employee safety.
- **Hardin's "Tragedy of the Commons"** — applies to shared security resources in unstable regions.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Framework Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet — Three-Challenge Matrix:**

| Dimension | Environment | Cyber | Violence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Ethical Theory** | Sustainability Ethics | Information Ethics | Human Rights / Duty of Care |
| **Key International Standard** | Paris Agreement / ISO 14001 | GDPR / NIST CSF | UNGPs / ISO 45001 |
| **Key Stakeholder** | Host community & future generations | Data subjects & consumers | Employees & local citizens |
| **Manager's Core Obligation** | Polluter Pays Principle | Privacy & Data Protection | Duty of Care |
| **Disclosure Framework** | ESG / GRI Standards | Privacy Impact Assessment | Security & Risk Reports |
| **Potential Penalty Magnitude** | Fines + Cleanup Costs | **€20M** / **4% turnover** | Litigation + Reputational Loss |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility for KTU Engineering-Business Students

For B.Tech graduates who may join **MNCs, IT companies, or manufacturing firms with overseas operations**, this framework equips them to:
- Make ethical decisions when host-nation laws are laxer than home-nation laws.
- Recognize the "**race to the bottom**" trap (where firms exploit weak regulations).
- Design CSR programs that address genuine local needs rather than corporate PR.
- Lead crisis-response teams during environmental disasters, cyberattacks, or civil unrest.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Logic

### 3.1 The Integrated Ethical Decision-Making Model for International Managers

Because the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **application-level questions (Bloom Level 3–4)**, students must demonstrate the *process* of ethical decision-making in international contexts. The following model is the **gold-standard framework** taught in this module.

---

#### Step 1 — Problem Identification

The manager must clearly articulate **what** is happening, **who** is affected, and **where** (which jurisdiction).

$$
\text{Problem Statement} = f(\text{Facts}, \text{Stakeholders}, \text{Jurisdiction})
$$

**Example:** A textile MNC in Bangladesh discharges untreated dye effluent into the Buriganga River. The river crosses into India, harming downstream farmers.

- **Facts:** Untreated effluent discharge.
- **Stakeholders:** Local community, downstream farmers, regulators, shareholders.
- **Jurisdictions:** Bangladesh (host) + India (affected) + MNC's home country.

---

#### Step 2 — Stakeholder Mapping

Using **Mitchell's Agility-Stakeholder Salience Model**, classify stakeholders by **Power**, **Legitimacy**, and **Urgency**.

$$
\text{Salience} = \text{Power} \times \text{Legitimacy} \times \text{Urgency}
$$

- **Definitive stakeholders** (high on all three): Host community, regulators.
- **Dependent stakeholders** (high legitimacy, low power): Downstream farmers.
- **Dangerous stakeholders** (high power, low legitimacy): Corrupt local officials.

---

#### Step 3 — Application of Ethical Lenses

Apply **multiple ethical theories** to evaluate the situation:

$$
\text{Ethical Decision} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E_i
$$

Where $E_i$ is the recommendation from theory $i$, and $w_i$ is the contextual weight.

| Theory | Recommendation for the Bangladeshi Dye Case |
|---|---|
| **Utilitarianism** | Stop discharge — maximizes welfare of the largest number (farmers > factory profits). |
| **Deontology (Kant)** | Treating the river as a means to profit violates the river-ecosystem's intrinsic worth. |
| **Virtue Ethics** | A virtuous manager does not poison communities for margin. |
| **Rights Theory** | Downstream farmers have a right to clean water (UDHR Art. 25). |
| **Polluter Pays Principle** | MNC must bear full cleanup cost. |

---

#### Step 4 — Legal & Regulatory Cross-Check

$$
\text{Compliance Score} = \frac{\text{Standards Met}}{\text{Total Applicable Standards}} \times 100\%
$$

For the dye case:

| Standard | Applicable? | Met? |
|---|---|---|
| Bangladesh Environment Conservation Act, 1995 | Yes | ❌ |
| UN SDG 6 (Clean Water) | Yes | ❌ |
| ISO 14001 EMS | Voluntary | ❌ |
| Home-country Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) | Yes (if bribery involved) | ✓ |

---

#### Step 5 — Decision & Implementation

Choose the action that satisfies **ethical, legal, and strategic** criteria simultaneously.

$$
\text{Optimal Action} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \; [\alpha \cdot E(a) + \beta \cdot L(a) + \gamma \cdot S(a)]
$$

Where:
- $E(a)$ = ethical score of action $a$
- $L(a)$ = legal compliance score
- $S(a)$ = strategic value (long-term)
- $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ and represent managerial priority weights.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Challenge Domains

The following matrix is **highly examinable** in KTU questions:

| Analytical Lens | Environmental | Cyber | Violence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Harm Type** | Externalities | Information asymmetry | Physical / psychological harm |
| **Time Horizon of Impact** | Long-term (years/decades) | Immediate to medium-term | Immediate |
| **Reversibility** | Often irreversible | Reversible with backups | Mostly irreversible |
| **Visible vs. Invisible** | Often invisible | Largely invisible | Highly visible |
| **Who Bears the Cost?** | Host community & future generations | Data subjects & customers | Employees & locals |
| **Best Ethical Theory** | Sustainability / Intergenerational Equity | Information Ethics / Privacy Rights | Duty of Care / Human Rights |
| **Risk Management Standard** | ISO 14001 | ISO 27001 / NIST CSF | ISO 45001 |
| **Disclosure Requirement** | ESG / Sustainability Report | Data Breach Notification Law | Security & Incident Report |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Cyber Challenge

> **Scenario:** An Indian IT services firm handles customer data of a German bank. A phishing attack compromises **50,000** customer records. As international manager, what is your course of action?

**Step-by-Step Resolution:**

1. **Identify Breach** — Detection of unauthorized access at **T = 0 hours**.
2. **Containment** — Isolate affected servers within **72 hours** (GDPR requirement).
3. **Notification to Supervisory Authority** — Within **72 hours** under GDPR Art. 33.
4. **Notification to Data Subjects** — Without undue delay if high risk (GDPR Art. 34).
5. **Forensic Investigation** — Engage cybersecurity firm.
6. **Remediation** — Patch vulnerabilities, reset credentials, offer credit monitoring.
7. **Documentation & Reporting** — Maintain records per **GDPR Art. 30**.
8. **Learning & Process Improvement** — Update SOC (Security Operations Center) procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure to comply with GDPR may invite penalties up to €20 million or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics
