> **Conclusion:** Indian CSR law is a **landmark** in global corporate governance — it embeds social responsibility into the very **statutory DNA** of qualifying corporations, making CSR a **compliance obligation** rather than a discretionary choice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on CSR questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Conflating "Legal" and "Mandatory" CSR:** Section 135 applies only to companies crossing the prescribed thresholds (Net Worth ≥ ₹500 Cr / Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Cr / Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr). Smaller companies are **not** bound by 2% spend.
>
> 2. **❌ Listing Carroll's 4 types without explaining the "hierarchy":** Examiners award full marks only when students explain **why** economic is at the base and philanthropic at the top.
>
> 3. **❌ Confusing "Philanthropy" with "CSR":** Philanthropy is **one type** of CSR, not a synonym. CSR is the broader umbrella.
>
> 4. **❌ Forgetting the 3-year average profit formula:** Use $\frac{\text{NP}_{\text{FY1}} + \text{NP}_{\text{FY2}} + \text{NP}_{\text{FY3}}}{3}$, **not** the latest year's profit alone.
>
> 5. **❌ Omitting Indian examples:** KTU examiners reward **India-specific case studies** (Tata, Infosys, Wipro, ITC, Reliance Foundation) over generic Western examples.
>
> 6. **❌ Ignoring the legal / penalty angle:** If a qualifying company fails to spend the 2%, the unspent amount must be transferred within **30 days** to the **Unspent CSR Account**, and if unutilized in 3 years, to Schedule VII funds. Many students omit this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision — Meaning and Types of CSR (Module 4)**

- [x] **CSR** = balancing profit with social, environmental, and ethical obligations to all stakeholders.
- [x] **Carroll's Pyramid (4 Types):** **E**conomic → **L**egal → **E**thical → **P**hilanthropic (Base → Top).
- [x] **Mnemonic:** *"E²-L-P"* (Economic, Ethical, Legal, Philanthropic).
- [x] **Legal = "Must do"** | **Ethical = "Should do"** | **Philanthropic = "Want to do."**
- [x] **Section 135 of Companies Act, 2013** mandates **2% CSR spend** for qualifying companies.
- [x] **Formula:** $\text{CSR} = 2\% \times \text{Average Net Profit (Last 3 Years)}$.
- [x] **Applicability threshold:** Net Worth ≥ ₹500 Cr **OR** Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Cr **OR** Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr.
- [x] **Governance:** CSR Committee (≥3 directors, ≥1 independent director).
- [x] **Schedule VII** lists 11 broad categories of permissible CSR activities.
- [x] **Key Indian Examples:** Tata Trusts (education/health), TCS (digital literacy), Wipro (carbon neutrality), Infosys (rural development), ITC (e-Choupal).
- [x] **Evolution:** Bowen (1953) → Davis (Iron Law) → CED → Carroll's Pyramid (1979) → Freeman (Stakeholder Theory, 1984) → Porter & Kramer (Shared Value, 1990s) → ISO 26000 (2011) → Section 135 (2013).
- [x] **Triple Bottom Line (TBL):** **People, Planet, Profit.**
- [x] **Types beyond Carroll:** Traditional, Strategic, Ethical, Environmental (Green), Inward, Outward.
- [x] **Penalty for non-spend:** Transfer unspent to **Unspent CSR Account** (30 days) → use in 3 years, else transfer to **PM CARES / Schedule VII fund**.
- [x] **Key Distinction:** CSR ≠ Charity. CSR is a **strategic business approach**, not mere philanthropy.
- [x] **Stakeholders (7-ring model):** Employees, Customers, Shareholders, Suppliers, Community, Government, Environment.

> **Final Exam Mantra for this Topic:** *"Begin with a precise definition, build around Carroll's 4-tier pyramid, ground it in Section 135 of the Indian Companies Act, and seal it with a Tata or Infosys example."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of CSR Towards Society: Employees, Government, Stakeholders, and Consumers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT756 - BUSINESS ETHICS AND CORPORATE GOVERNANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Role of CSR Towards Society: Employees, Government, Stakeholders, and Consumers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a company to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society, including **economic, social, and environmental** dimensions.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (Module 4 of PECBT756), the *Role of CSR Towards Society* is examined through four primary stakeholder lenses:

1. **Employees (Internal Stakeholders)**
2. **Government (Regulatory Stakeholder)**
3. **Stakeholders (Broader Community — Shareholders, NGOs, Society at large)**
4. **Consumers (Market Stakeholders)**

The **European Commission (2011)** formally defines CSR as:
> *"The responsibility of enterprises for their impacts on society. To fully meet their corporate social responsibility, enterprises should have in place a process to integrate social, environmental, ethical, human rights, and consumer concerns into their business operations and core strategy."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Good Citizen Company" Analogy**:
> Imagine a company as a person living in a large neighborhood (society). Just as a good neighbor:
> - Keeps their house safe and pays its workers fairly (**Employees**)
> - Follows the town's laws and pays municipal taxes (**Government**)
> - Contributes to community parks, schools, and local charities (**Stakeholders**)
> - Does not sell harmful or defective products to neighbors (**Consumers**)
>
> ...a socially responsible company must behave ethically toward *each* of these groups, balancing profit with purpose.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **2% of Average Net Profit** | Mandatory CSR spend under Section 135, Companies Act 2013 (India) | **Companies Act 2013** |
| **ISO 26000** | International standard for Social Responsibility | **ISO 26000:2010** |
| **GRI Standards** | Global Reporting Initiative for sustainability reporting | **GRI (2021 update)** |
| **ESG Score** | Environmental, Social, Governance rating | **MSCI / S&P DJSI** |
| **B Corp Certification** | B Lab certification for social/environmental performance | **B Lab Global** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU examiners frequently quote Section 135 of the Indian Companies Act 2013, which mandates CSR spending of **at least 2% of the average net profits** of the preceding three financial years for companies meeting specific turnover/profit thresholds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Archie B. Carroll's CSR Pyramid** is the most cited framework in KTU examinations for this module. It organizes CSR responsibilities into four hierarchical levels:

$$
\text{Total CSR Responsibility} = \text{Economic} + \text{Legal} + \text{Ethical} + \text{Philanthropic}
$$

| Layer | Responsibility | Description | Example Toward Society |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Base** | **Economic** | Be profitable — the foundational duty | Provide jobs, generate wealth, pay fair wages to **Employees** |
| **2. Second** | **Legal** | Obey the law — comply with regulations | Pay taxes honestly to **Government**, follow labor laws |
| **3. Third** | **Ethical** | Do what is right, just, and fair — beyond legal compliance | Transparent reporting to **Stakeholders**, fair pricing for **Consumers** |
| **4. Top** | **Philanthropic** | Be a good corporate citizen — contribute to community welfare | Donations, volunteering, environmental programs |

---

### 2.2 Role of CSR Toward Each Stakeholder Group

#### A. Role of CSR Toward **Employees**

Employees are the **primary internal stakeholders**. CSR initiatives here focus on their well-being, growth, and dignity.

**Core Responsibilities:**
- **Fair Wages & Equal Pay** — comply with minimum wage laws and bridge gender pay gaps.
- **Safe Working Conditions** — occupational health and safety (OHS) standards.
- **Skill Development & Training** — upskilling programs, leadership development.
- **Work-Life Balance** — flexible hours, parental leave, mental health support.
- **Non-Discrimination** — diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) policies.
- **Whistleblower Protection** — ethical grievance redressal mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Example**: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) runs the *"TCS Sustainability Assessment"* and was awarded the *Top Employer in Europe* for employee welfare.

#### B. Role of CSR Toward **Government**

The Government acts as the **regulatory stakeholder** that defines the legal operating environment.

**Core Responsibilities:**
- **Tax Compliance** — pay corporate taxes, GST, and statutory dues honestly.
- **Adherence to Labor & Environmental Laws** — Factories Act, Pollution Control norms.
- **Transparency in Disclosures** — timely filing of annual reports, audited financials.
- **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)** — collaborate on national development projects.
- **Employment Generation** — contribute to national employment goals.
- **Compliance with Section 135 (CSR Mandate)** — for eligible companies under Indian law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Mandate**: Under **Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013**, every company with:
> - Net worth ≥ **₹500 crore**, OR
> - Turnover ≥ **₹1000 crore**, OR
> - Net profit ≥ **₹5 crore**
>
> must spend at least **2% of average net profits of the preceding 3 years** on CSR activities.

#### C. Role of CSR Toward **Stakeholders (Society at large)**

This is the **broadest interpretation** of CSR and includes shareholders, NGOs, local communities, civil society, and future generations.

**Core Responsibilities:**
- **Community Development** — rural development, education, healthcare, sanitation.
- **Environmental Stewardship** — carbon footprint reduction, waste management, water conservation.
- **Ethical Supply Chain Management** — no child labor, fair trade sourcing.
- **Investor Relations** — transparent ESG disclosures, ethical governance.
- **Philanthropy & Volunteering** — corporate foundations, employee volunteering programs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Example**: Infosys Foundation — runs programs in *rural sanitation, education, and arts* (e.g., the *Arun Jaitley Centre of Excellence*).

#### D. Role of CSR Toward **Consumers**

Consumers are the **market stakeholders** who ultimately determine a company's commercial success.

**Core Responsibilities:**
- **Product Safety & Quality** — adherence to BIS/ISO standards.
- **Honest Marketing & Labeling** — no misleading advertisements.
- **Fair Pricing** — no price gouging, especially during crises.
- **Data Privacy & Security** — protection of consumer data (especially for IT/fintech).
- **Effective Grievance Redressal** — accessible customer service channels.
- **Sustainable Products** — eco-friendly packaging, recyclable materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Example**: Hindustan Unilever's *Pureit* water purifier — provides safe drinking water at affordable prices to rural consumers, blending CSR with product design.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Unit / Threshold | Applicable Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mandatory CSR Spend (India) | $\text{CSR Spend} \geq 0.02 \times \text{Avg. Net Profit (3 yrs)}$ | **% of Profit** | Government / Society |
| Carroll's Total Responsibility | $T_{CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph$ | Dimensionless score | All stakeholders |
| ESG Composite Score | $S_{ESG} = w_E \cdot E + w_S \cdot S + w_G \cdot G$ | 0–100 scale | Investors / Society |
| Net Profit Threshold (Section 135) | $NP \geq \text{₹ 5 Crore}$ | INR | Government |
| Carbon Intensity (CSR Metric) | $CI = \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions (tonnes)}}{\text{Revenue (₹ Crore)}}$ | Tonnes/₹ Crore | Society / Environment |
| Pay Ratio Disclosure (CSR) | $R_{pay} = \frac{\text{CEO Pay}}{\text{Median Employee Pay}}$ | Ratio | Employees / Society |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: While this is a management course, students should remember that *systems thinking* about stakeholders applies to all engineering project management — the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** of *People, Planet, Profit* is directly analogous to sustainability metrics in civil, mechanical, and environmental engineering.

---

### 2.4 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Framework (Elkington, 1994)

$$
\text{Sustainability} = f(\text{Social}, \text{Environmental}, \text{Economic})
$$

| Dimension | Stakeholder Mapping |
|---|---|
| **Social (People)** | Employees, Consumers, Local Communities |
| **Environmental (Planet)** | Society at large, Future Generations, Government (via NDCs) |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Shareholders, Government (via taxes), Employees (via wages) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Stakeholder Mapping & CSR Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is **non-quantitative management**, "derivations" here refer to structured analytical frameworks that logically connect CSR actions to stakeholder outcomes. Every step is expanded for KTU exam-style answers.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: CSR Role × Stakeholder Group

The following **8-step analytical framework** systematically maps CSR to each stakeholder:

**Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification**
A company must first *identify* all its stakeholders using the **Power/Interest Matrix** (Mendelow, 1991):

$$
\text{Priority} = f(\text{Power}, \text{Interest})
$$

- High Power + High Interest = **Manage Closely** (e.g., Government, Major Shareholders)
- High Power + Low Interest = **Keep Satisfied** (e.g., Regulators)
- Low Power + High Interest = **Keep Informed** (e.g., NGOs, Employees)
- Low Power + Low Interest = **Monitor** (e.g., General Public)

**Step 2 — Stakeholder Engagement Strategy**
For each group, the company designs a **CSR engagement plan**:
- Employees → Internal CSR committees, employee welfare budgets
- Government → Compliance audits, PPPs
- Stakeholders → Community outreach, sustainability reports
- Consumers → Quality certifications, customer care

**Step 3 — CSR Budget Allocation Logic**
Total CSR budget is split based on **stakeholder salience**:

$$
B_i = B_{total} \times \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} S_j}
$$

Where:
- $B_i$ = Budget allocated to stakeholder group $i$
- $S_i$ = Salience score of stakeholder group $i$
- $B_{total}$ = Total annual CSR budget

**Step 4 — Employee-Focused CSR Calculation (Workplace Diversity Index)**

$$
D_{index} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k \cdot d_k}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k}
$$

Where $d_k$ is the diversity ratio in category $k$ (e.g., gender, ethnicity, disability) and $w_k$ is its assigned weight. A higher index reflects stronger CSR toward employees.

**Step 5 — Government-Focused CSR Verification (CSR Mandate Compliance)**

$$
C_{mandate} = \frac{\text{Actual CSR Spend}}{\text{Required CSR Spend} = 0.02 \times \overline{NP}_3}
$$

If $C_{mandate} \geq 1.0$, the company complies with Section 135.

**Step 6 — Consumer-Focused CSR Quality Score**

$$
Q_{consumer} = \alpha \cdot S_{product} + \beta \cdot R_{service} + \gamma \cdot P_{privacy}
$$

Where $S$ = Safety, $R$ = Reliability, $P$ = Privacy, and $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

**Step 7 — Stakeholder (Society) Impact Measurement (SROI)**

$$
\text{SROI} = \frac{\text{Net Present Value of Social Impact (₹)}}{\text{Net Present Value of CSR Investment (₹)}}
$$

An SROI > 1 indicates positive social value creation.

**Step 8 — Integrated CSR Reporting (GRI / BRSR Framework)**
The final step is consolidating all stakeholder CSR activities into a **Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR)** — now mandatory for top 1000 listed Indian companies by SEBI.

---

### 3.2 Worked Conceptual Example (KTU-Style)

**Scenario**: A manufacturing company *XYZ Ltd.* has an average net profit of ₹100 crore over the last 3 years.

**Step A**: Calculate mandatory CSR spend:

$$
\text{CSR Spend}_{mandatory} = 0.02 \times 100 = ₹2 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step B**: Allocate the ₹2 Crore across stakeholders:

| Stakeholder | Salience | Allocation (₹) | Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| Employees | 0.30 | 60,00,000 | Safety training, daycare |
| Government (PPP) | 0.15 | 30,00,000 | Smart village infrastructure |
| Society/Community | 0.35 | 70,00,000 | Education & healthcare |
| Consumers | 0.20 | 40,00,000 | Quality testing lab |
| **Total** | **1.00** | **₹2,00,00,000** | — |

**Step C**: Verify compliance:

$$
C_{mandate} = \frac{₹2,00,00,000}{₹2,00,00,000} = 1.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Fully Compliant}
$$

**Step D**: Compute SROI if the ₹2 Crore creates ₹5 Crore in measurable social value:

$$
\text{SROI} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5
$$

This means **every ₹1 invested created ₹2.50 of social value** — a strong CSR outcome suitable for KTU case-based answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CSR Stakeholder Ecosystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CSR Strategy Core]:::core --> B[Employees]
    A --> C[Government]
    A --> D[Stakeholders - Society]
    A --> E[Consumers]
    B --> B1[Fair Wages]
    B --> B2[Safe Workplace]
    B --> B3[Training & DEI]
    C --> C1[Tax Compliance]
    C --> C2[Section 135 Spend]
    C --> C3[PPP Projects]
    D --> D1[Community Development]
    D --> D2[Environment Care]
    D --> D3[Ethical Sourcing]
    E --> E1[Product Safety]
    E --> E2[Fair Pricing]
    E --> E3[Data Privacy]
    B1 --> F1[Triple Bottom Line: People]
    B2 --> F1
    C1 --> F2[Triple Bottom Line: Profit]
    C2 --> F2
    D2 --> F3[Triple Bottom Line: Planet]
    E1 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G[Holistic CSR Impact]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Carroll's CSR Pyramid (Stacked Layout)

```mermaid
graph BT
    P1[Philanthropic Responsibility]:::top
    P2[Ethical Responsibility]:::third
    P3[Legal Responsibility]:::second
    P4[Economic Responsibility]:::base
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
    P1 --> STK[Beneficiaries: Employees, Government, Stakeholders, Consumers]:::stk
    classDef base fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef second fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef third fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef stk fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: CSR Decision Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Company Operations Begin]) --> Step1[Identify Stakeholders via Power-Interest Matrix]
    Step1 --> Step2{Company Eligible under Section 135?}
    Step2 -- Yes --> Step3[Calculate 2% of Avg Net Profit]
    Step2 -- No --> Step4[Proceed with Voluntary CSR]
    Step3 --> Step5[Design CSR Programs per Stakeholder]
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6[Allocate Budget: 4 Buckets]
    Step6 --> Step61[Employee Welfare 30%]
    Step6 --> Step62[Government & PPP 15%]
    Step6 --> Step63[Community Society 35%]
    Step6 --> Step64[Consumer Trust 20%]
    Step61 --> Step7[Execute and Monitor Programs]
    Step62 --> Step7
    Step63 --> Step7
    Step64 --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8[Publish BRSR and ESG Reports]
    Step8 --> Step9{Meets Compliance and SROI Threshold?}
    Step9 -- Yes --> Step10[Certified as Socially Responsible Enterprise]
    Step9 -- No --> Step11[Corrective Action Plan]
    Step11 --> Step7
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Power-Interest Grid for Stakeholder Prioritization

```mermaid
graph LR
    HighP[High Power] --> HP_HI[Manage Closely: Government, Major Investors]
    HighP --> HP_LI[Keep Satisfied: Regulators, Tax Authorities]
    LowP[Low Power] --> LP_HI[Keep Informed: NGOs, Employees, Local Community]
    LowP --> LP_LI[Monitor: General Consumers, Media]
    HP_HI --> Action1[Quarterly Engagement]
    HP_LI --> Action2[Annual Compliance Audit]
    LP_HI --> Action3[Monthly CSR Reports]
    LP_LI --> Action4[Periodic Surveys]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: The four-quadrant Power-Interest matrix above is a **block-level functional map** showing *who* a company should engage with *how often* — a question frequently asked in KTU 14-mark essay questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define Corporate Social Responsibility. Mention the mandatory CSR spend requirement under the Indian Companies Act 2013.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a management concept whereby companies integrate social and environmental concerns into their business operations and interactions with stakeholders on a voluntary basis. Under **Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013**, every company meeting specified thresholds (net worth ≥ ₹500 crore, turnover ≥ ₹1000 crore, or net profit ≥ ₹5 crore) is required to spend at least **2% of the average net profits of the preceding three financial years** on CSR activities. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Section 135, 1 for the 2% rule)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** List any three responsibilities of a company towards its consumers under CSR.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A company's CSR responsibilities toward consumers include:
1. **Product Safety and Quality** — ensuring goods meet prescribed safety standards (e.g., BIS certification).
2. **Honest Marketing and Labeling** — avoiding misleading advertisements and disclosing product information transparently.
3. **Data Privacy Protection** — safeguarding consumer personal data, especially in digital/IT businesses.
*(1 mark each — total 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Choose either A OR B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Essay Style*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the role of CSR toward **employees and government** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Role toward Employees (4 marks):**
- **Fair Wages and Benefits** — Companies must pay statutory minimum wages and ensure parity; e.g., *Infosys* is known for industry-leading compensation and ESOPs.
- **Workplace Safety** — Compliance with the Factories Act and OHSAS/ISO 45001; e.g., *Reliance Industries* invests in safety drills and PPE.
- **Training & Development** — Continuous learning programs; e.g., *TCS' Learning Wings* and *Wipro's Mission10X*.
- **Diversity, Equity & Inclusion (DEI)** — Equal opportunity employer policies; e.g., *Accenture India* employing 50%+ women in workforce.

**Role toward Government (3 marks):**
- **Tax Compliance** — Honest payment of corporate tax, GST, TDS, etc.
- **Adherence to Section 135** — Mandatory 2% CSR spending for eligible firms.
- **Public-Private Partnerships** — e.g., *Adani Group* partnering with the *Government of India* on the GIFT City smart infrastructure project.
- **Timely Disclosures** — Filing of annual returns, BRSR reports, and SEBI compliance.

---

**(b)** Discuss the role of CSR toward **stakeholders and consumers**. How does Carroll's Pyramid support these roles? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Role toward Stakeholders / Society (3 marks):**
- **Community Development** — Education, healthcare, sanitation; e.g., *Tata Steel*'s *Arogyam* rural health program.
- **Environmental Stewardship** — Carbon-neutral goals; e.g., *Wipro* achieving 100% renewable energy.
- **Ethical Supply Chain** — Fair trade, no child labor; e.g., *H&M's* transparency in Bangladesh sourcing.

**Role toward Consumers (2 marks):**
- **Product Quality & Safety** — e.g., *Maruti Suzuki*'s recall mechanisms.
- **Data Privacy** — e.g., *HDFC Bank*'s secure digital banking practices.
- **Fair Pricing** — No cartelization; adherence to Competition Act 2002.

**Carroll's Pyramid Connection (2 marks):**
- **Economic** layer → Job creation for employees, fair prices for consumers.
- **Legal** layer → Tax payment to government, compliance with consumer protection laws.
- **Ethical** layer → Transparent ESG reporting to stakeholders.
- **Philanthropic** layer → Community welfare programs benefiting society.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Case-Based / Analytical Style*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *Case Study: XYZ Ltd. is an Indian IT company with a 3-year average net profit of ₹800 crore. The company employs 50,000 people and operates in 20 countries. Recently, it faced criticism for high attrition and a data breach affecting 1 million customers.*
**Analyze the CSR obligations of XYZ Ltd. toward (i) employees and (ii) consumers, with reference to applicable laws and frameworks. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) CSR toward Employees (3.5 marks):**
- With ₹800 crore net profit, XYZ Ltd. **qualifies under Section 135** (profit > ₹5 crore).
- **Mandatory CSR spend** = $0.02 \times 800 = ₹16$ Crore.
- High attrition signals poor **employee welfare CSR** — needs investment in mental health, work-life balance, upskilling.
- Should implement **DEI policies, whistleblower mechanisms, fair performance-based pay**.
- Reference: **Industries (Standing Orders) Act 1946**, **POSH Act 2013**, and **OSHA-equivalent** standards.

**(ii) CSR toward Consumers (3.5 marks):**
- The data breach violates **consumer trust** and the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDP Act)**.
- Company must:
  - Compensate affected consumers
  - Invest in **cybersecurity infrastructure** as a CSR-ethics move
  - Disclose breach transparently
  - Appoint a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)**
- Reference: **Consumer Protection Act 2019**, **IT Act 2000**, **DPDP Act 2023**.

---

**(b)** *Using the same case, suggest how XYZ Ltd. can fulfill its CSR obligations toward the **Government and broader stakeholders (society)**. Also explain the relevance of the **Triple Bottom Line** framework. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Government (3 marks):**
- Pay corporate taxes and **2% CSR = ₹16 Crore** to comply with Section 135.
- Partner with government on **Digital India** and **Skill India** programs.
- File **BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report)** as mandated by SEBI.
- Ensure **FCRA compliance** for any foreign CSR contributions.

**Society / Stakeholders (2 marks):**
- Launch **community skilling centers** in rural India (utilizing CSR funds).
- Adopt **renewable energy** in data centers — supports *net-zero* commitment.
- Engage NGOs in monitoring and impact assessment.

**Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Elkington (2 marks):**
- **People (Social)**: Employees, consumers, local community.
- **Planet (Environmental)**: Carbon footprint, e-waste management.
- **Profit (Economic)**: Shareholders, government (via taxes).
- TBL aligns with **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** — especially SDG 8 (Decent Work) and SDG 13 (Climate Action).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Scheme)**:
>
> 1. **Confusing CSR with Charity** — CSR is a *strategic, integrated* business practice, *not* ad-hoc donations. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you equate CSR to mere philanthropy.
> 2. **Forgetting Section 135 Mandate** — When the question mentions "Indian company", examiners expect the **2% of average net profit** rule to be cited. Missing it = lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Vague Examples** — "Companies do CSR" is not enough. Use **specific company names + specific programs** (e.g., *Tata Steel's Arogyam*, *Infosys Foundation's Mangalore Seva*).
> 4. **Ignoring Carroll's Pyramid / TBL** — These are the *most-asked frameworks* in Part B. Always cite at least one.
> 5. **No Stakeholder Mapping** — When asked about "society", students often forget to subdivide into *employees, government, shareholders, NGOs, community*.
> 6. **Missing BRSR / ESG References** — For 2024 scheme, mentioning **BRSR, GRI, ESG, or SDG** frameworks fetches bonus marks.
> 7. **Spelling Mistakes** — "Stakeholders" vs "Shareholders" confusion is a common error — examiners deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition**: CSR = Economic + Legal + Ethical + Philanthropic responsibilities (Carroll, 1991).
- **Statute**: **Section 135, Companies Act 2013** → mandatory **2% of 3-year average net profit**.
- **Thresholds** (any one): Net worth ≥ ₹500 Cr **OR** Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Cr **OR** Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr.
- **Toward Employees**: Fair wages, safety, training, DEI, grievance redressal.
- **Toward Government**: Tax compliance, PPPs, Section 135 spend, BRSR filing.
- **Toward Stakeholders/Society**: Community development, environment, ethical sourcing, ESG disclosure.
- **Toward Consumers**: Product safety, fair pricing, data privacy, grievance redressal.
- **Frameworks to remember**:
  - Carroll's **CSR Pyramid** (1991)
  - Elkington's **Triple Bottom Line** (1994)
  - Mendelow's **Power-Interest Matrix** (1991)
  - Freeman's **Stakeholder Theory** (1984)
- **Standards/Reports**:
  - **ISO 26000** (Social Responsibility)
  - **GRI Standards** (Sustainability Reporting)
  - **BRSR** (SEBI, India) — *mandatory for top 1000 listed companies*
  - **ESG Scores** (MSCI, S&P DJSI, Sustainalytics)
- **Key Indian Examples**:
  - *Tata Group* (Tata Steel Arogyam, TCS Best Employer)
  - *Infosys Foundation* (Rural sanitation & education)
  - *Wipro* (100% renewable energy — Earthian program)
  - *Hindustan Unilever Pureit* (Affordable safe drinking water)
- **International Example**: *Patagonia* (1% of sales donated to environmental causes), *Microsoft* (carbon negative by 2030).
- **SDG Linkages**: SDG 4 (Education), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), SDG 8 (Decent Work), SDG 13 (Climate Action).
- **Mandatory Reporting**: BRSR Core for top 1000 listed Indian companies (SEBI circular 2023).
- **Formula Recall**:
  - CSR Spend = $0.02 \times \overline{NP}_3$
  - Total CSR = $E + L + Et + Ph$
  - SROI = $\frac{\text{NPV of Impact}}{\text{NPV of Investment}}$
- **Key Tagline for KTU Answers**: *"A responsible business is not just profitable — it is ethical, legal, and philanthropic."*

> **🎯 Pro Tip for 14-mark Answers**: Always structure your KTU Module 4 answer as:
> 1. **Introduction** (Carroll's definition — 2 lines)
> 2. **Body** (4 sub-sections — one per stakeholder, 2–3 marks each)
> 3. **Real-world example** (1 mark)
> 4. **Framework reference** (Carroll/TBL/Freeman — 1 mark)
> 5. **Conclusion** linking to **SDG/ESG/BRSR** (1 mark)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature of CSR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT756 - BUSINESS ETHICS AND CORPORATE GOVERNANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nature of CSR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a company to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society, including economically, socially, and environmentally.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT756) Module 4** terminology, CSR is formally defined as:

> **"The continuing commitment by business to behave ethically and contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce and their families as well as of the local community and society at large."**
> — *World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), Holme & Watts (2000)*

In the Indian statutory context, **Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013** defines CSR as the obligation of companies meeting specific financial thresholds to spend a percentage of their average net profits on activities enumerated in **Schedule VII** of the Act.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Good Citizen" Analogy**
> Imagine a person living in a neighborhood. If they only take from the community — using roads, electricity, water, public parks — but never contribute back (no taxes, no volunteer work, no helping neighbors), they would be considered a *bad citizen*. Over time, the community would resent them, and their quality of life would deteriorate.
>
> A **company is exactly like a citizen of society**. It draws resources (labor, raw materials, customers, infrastructure) from society. CSR is simply the company saying, *"I am a good citizen. I will give back more than I take."*

This is the **"License to Operate"** concept — society gives a company the right to do business, and in return, the company owes duties to society.

### 1.3 Key Operational Metrics

| Metric | Threshold / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandatory CSR Spend (India)** | **2% of average net profits** of the preceding 3 financial years | As per Companies Act 2013, Section 135(5) |
| **Applicability Criteria** | Net worth ≥ ₹500 Cr **OR** Turnover ≥ ₹1000 Cr **OR** Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr | Any one criterion triggers CSR obligation |
| **CSR Reporting Standard** | **CSR-2 Form** | Filed annually with MCA (Ministry of Corporate Affairs) |

### 1.4 Core Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Nature of CSR: Learning Anchors**
> - Understand the **scope and boundaries** of CSR activity.
> - Distinguish between CSR and related concepts (charity, philanthropy, sustainability).
> - Identify the **dimensions** of CSR (Economic, Legal, Ethical, Philanthropic).
> - Analyze **stakeholders** affected by CSR decisions.
> - Evaluate the **scope** of CSR across internal and external spheres.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Defining Characteristics (The "Nature") of CSR

The nature of CSR can be dissected into **six fundamental characteristics** that are repeatedly examined in KTU ESE questions:

1. **Voluntary yet Obligatory** — In the broader sense, CSR is voluntary; in India, it is legally mandatory for qualifying companies.
2. **Beyond Legal Compliance** — CSR goes *beyond* what law mandates; it is about ethical surplus.
3. **Stakeholder-Oriented** — Directs concern toward all stakeholders, not only shareholders.
4. **Integrated with Business Strategy** — Not a one-off activity; embedded in core operations.
5. **Long-term Horizon** — Focuses on sustainable, intergenerational impact.
6. **Multi-dimensional** — Encompasses economic, legal, ethical, and philanthropic responsibilities.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Logic

| Step | Why (Rationale) | How (Mechanism) |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1: Identify Stakeholders** | Business depends on a web of relationships | Map internal (employees, owners) and external (community, environment, government) parties |
| **Step 2: Assess Impact** | To know where harm or benefit occurs | Conduct materiality assessment, ESG audits |
| **Step 3: Set CSR Pillars** | To focus effort and budget | Choose from Schedule VII areas: education, health, environment, gender equality, rural development |
| **Step 4: Implement Programs** | Convert intent into action | Run in-house projects, partner with NGOs, fund Section 8 companies |
| **Step 5: Measure & Report** | Accountability and transparency | Publish annual CSR report in **CSR-2 format**, audited by independent agencies |
| **Step 6: Reinvest in Strategy** | Continuous improvement | Feed insights back into core business strategy |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — CSR Framework Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although CSR is a management topic (no algebraic equations), KTU examiners often test the **"Formula"** of CSR as conceptual frameworks. The table below serves as the **KTU high-yield formula sheet** for Module 4.

| Framework | Author / Year | Core "Formula" | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CSR Pyramid** | **Archie B. Carroll (1991)** | $\text{CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph$ | **E**conomic + **L**egal + **Et**hical + **Ph**ilanthropic responsibilities |
| **Three-Domain Model** | **Stephen Robbins** | $\text{CSR} = \text{Social} + \text{Economic} + \text{Ecological}$ | Tripartite bottom line / Triple Bottom Line (TBL) |
| **Stakeholder Model** | **R. Edward Freeman (1984)** | $\text{CSR} = f(\text{Stakeholders})$ | Duty to all who can affect or are affected by the firm |
| **Triple Bottom Line** | **John Elkington (1994)** | $\text{TBL} = \text{People} + \text{Planet} + \text{Profit}$ | Three Ps of sustainable business |
| **CSR Indian Mandate** | **Companies Act 2013, Sec 135** | $\text{CSR Spend} = 2\% \times \overline{NP}_3$ | $\overline{NP}_3$ = average net profit of last 3 FYs |
| **Mandatory Trigger** | Section 135(1) | $\text{Trigger} = (NW \geq 500\text{Cr}) \lor (TO \geq 1000\text{Cr}) \lor (NP \geq 5\text{Cr})$ | Any one condition suffices |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering & management practice:**
> - **Risk Mitigation** — Proactive CSR reduces regulatory penalties, environmental lawsuits, and community backlash (e.g., the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy** led to mandatory CSR laws).
> - **Brand Equity** — Companies with strong CSR (e.g., **Tata Group, Infosys, ITC**) command higher customer loyalty and ESG ratings.
> - **Talent Retention** — 70%+ of Gen-Z employees prefer employers with visible CSR/ESG commitments (LinkedIn Workforce Report).
> - **Investor Attraction** — ESG-focused funds (>$30 trillion globally as of 2023) screen companies on CSR metrics before investing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Carroll's CSR Pyramid

Carroll's framework is the **most-tested CSR model in KTU ESE**. Here is the complete step-by-step analytical breakdown.

**Step 1: State the Core Equation**

Carroll proposed that the total social responsibility of a business is the sum of four layered responsibilities. The conceptual equation is:

$$\text{Total CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph$$

Where each term has a specific economic, legal, ethical, or philanthropic role.

**Step 2: Decompose Each Layer**

**Layer 1 — Economic Responsibility (E)**

This is the foundational layer. A business exists to produce goods/services that society needs and to be profitable so it can sustain itself.

$$
E = \text{Revenue} - \text{Costs (including CSR Investment)}
$$

**Layer 2 — Legal Responsibility (L)**

Society codifies its expectations into laws. Businesses must operate within the legal framework.

$$
L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Compliance}_i \quad \text{where } i = \text{Labor Laws, Tax Laws, Environmental Laws, Consumer Laws, ...}
$$

**Layer 3 — Ethical Responsibility (Et)**

Beyond what is *legal*, there is what is *right*. Ethics includes fairness, transparency, and respect for stakeholders — activities not codified by law but expected by society.

$$
Et = \text{Stakeholder Fairness} + \text{Moral Conduct} + \text{Transparency}
$$

**Layer 4 — Philanthropic Responsibility (Ph)**

The apex of the pyramid. Voluntary contributions to community welfare — not demanded by law nor strictly required by ethics, but desired by society.

$$
Ph = \text{Charity} + \text{Community Development} + \text{Volunteerism}
$$

**Step 3: Order the Layers (Pyramid Structure)**

The pyramid ordering reflects a hierarchy of expectations:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Economic (Base)} \rightarrow \text{Legal} \rightarrow \text{Ethical} \rightarrow \text{Philanthropic (Apex)}}_{\text{Increasing voluntariness, decreasing obligation}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply to a Real Company (Tata Consultancy Services — TCS)**

TCS exemplifies all four layers:
- **E**: ₹2.40 lakh crore+ in revenue (FY 2023–24) — sustained profitability.
- **L**: Compliance with all SEBI, RBI, Companies Act 2013, and labor laws.
- **Et**: Equal-opportunity employer, gender-neutral pay policies, transparent governance.
- **Ph**: Spends well over the mandated 2% on education, health, and digital literacy programs.

**Step 5: Critical Evaluation (KTU-Favorite Discussion Point)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Criticism of Carroll's Pyramid:**
> - Modern theorists (e.g., **Philip Kotler, David Hess**) argue that the pyramid order is too rigid.
> - In today's stakeholder economy, **ethical** responsibility may be more fundamental than economic in some contexts (e.g., blood-tinged diamonds, sweat-shop labor).
> - The pyramid is **descriptive**, not **prescriptive** — it describes expectations, not priorities.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CSR Dimensions

The following **2x2 matrix** is a high-yield KTU tool for distinguishing the **nature of CSR** activities:

| Dimension | **Required by Law?** | **Expected by Society?** | Example Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Economic** | Indirectly (tax compliance) | Yes | Profitable operations, fair pricing |
| **Legal** | **Yes** | Yes | Filing taxes, paying minimum wages |
| **Ethical** | No | **Yes** | Avoiding child labor, fair supplier audits |
| **Philanthropic** | No | Desired (not expected) | Building schools, disaster relief donations |

### 3.3 Stakeholder Mapping — The "Sphere of CSR Influence"

CSR operates across three concentric spheres of business responsibility. The exhaustive expansion:

$$
\text{CSR Influence} = \{ \text{Internal Sphere} \} \cup \{ \text{External Sphere} \} \cup \{ \text{Global Sphere} \}
$$

**Internal Sphere** = Employees, Shareholders, Management, Trade Unions.

$$
\text{Internal CSR} = \text{Employee Welfare} + \text{Health \& Safety} + \text{Skill Development}
$$

**External Sphere** = Customers, Suppliers, Local Community, NGOs, Government.

$$
\text{External CSR} = \text{Community Development} + \text{Supplier Ethics} + \text{Customer Care}
$$

**Global Sphere** = Environment, Future Generations, International Bodies.

$$
\text{Global CSR} = \text{Sustainability} + \text{Carbon Footprint Reduction} + \text{Human Rights}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question on "Nature of CSR" almost always expects you to (a) define CSR, (b) list its characteristics, and (c) explain any **one** framework (Carroll's pyramid is the safest choice for full marks).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carroll's CSR Pyramid — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    base["LAYER 1 - ECONOMIC RESPONSIBILITY"]:::econ
    layer2["LAYER 2 - LEGAL RESPONSIBILITY"]:::legal
    layer3["LAYER 3 - ETHICAL RESPONSIBILITY"]:::ethical
    apex["LAYER 4 - PHILANTHROPIC RESPONSIBILITY"]:::phil

    base --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> apex

    note1["Profit, ROI, Cost efficiency, Sustainable operations"]:::note
    note2["Lawful conduct, Tax compliance, Labor law adherence"]:::note
    note3["Fairness, Transparency, Beyond legal duty"]:::note
    note4["Voluntary giving, Community uplift, Donations"]:::note

    base -.- note1
    layer2 -.- note2
    layer3 -.- note3
    apex -.- note4

    classDef econ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef legal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ethical fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef phil fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#808080,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-style:italic
```

### 4.2 Stakeholder Sphere Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    firm["THE FIRM AT THE CENTER"]:::firm
    internal["INTERNAL SPHERE"]:::internal
    external["EXTERNAL SPHERE"]:::external
    global["GLOBAL SPHERE"]:::global

    employees["Employees and Workers"]:::stake
    owners["Shareholders and Owners"]:::stake
    managers["Managers and Board"]:::stake

    customers["Customers"]:::stake
    suppliers["Suppliers and Vendors"]:::stake
    community["Local Community"]:::stake
    govt["Government and Regulators"]:::stake
    ngos["NGOs and Civil Society"]:::stake

    environment["Natural Environment"]:::stake
    future["Future Generations"]:::stake
    intlbodies["International Bodies UN WHO ILO"]:::stake

    firm --- internal
    firm --- external
    firm --- global

    internal --- employees
    internal --- owners
    internal --- managers

    external --- customers
    external --- suppliers
    external --- community
    external --- govt
    external --- ngos

    global --- environment
    global --- future
    global --- intlbodies

    classDef firm fill:#000000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef external fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef global fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stake fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 CSR Implementation Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    a["STEP 1 Eligibility Check Sec 135"]:::step
    b["STEP 2 CSR Committee Formation"]:::step
    c["STEP 3 Budget Allocation 2 percent of NP"]:::step
    d["STEP 4 Activity Selection Schedule VII"]:::step
    e["STEP 5 Implementation In-house NGO Partner"]:::step
    f["STEP 6 Monitoring and Impact Assessment"]:::step
    g["STEP 7 Reporting CSR-2 Form to MCA"]:::step

    a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f --> g
    g -.Feedback Loop.-> a

    classDef step fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** The feedback loop in the implementation flow represents the **continuous improvement cycle** of CSR — the annual learnings feed back into revised strategies, making CSR a **dynamic, evolving** process rather than a one-time compliance activity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Corporate Social Responsibility. List any four characteristics of CSR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is a self-regulating business model whereby a company integrates social, environmental, and ethical concerns into its operations and stakeholder interactions, going beyond mere legal compliance to contribute positively to societal welfare.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Voluntary in Spirit, Mandatory in Law** — In India, qualifying companies must spend 2% of average net profits under Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013.
2. **Stakeholder-Oriented** — Concerns extend beyond shareholders to employees, customers, community, and environment.
3. **Beyond Compliance** — Encompasses ethical and philanthropic duties that exceed legal minimums.
4. **Long-term and Sustainable** — Focuses on intergenerational welfare and sustainable development goals (SDGs).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between CSR and philanthropy with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **CSR** | **Philanthropy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broad — economic, legal, ethical, philanthropic | Narrow — voluntary giving only |
| **Legal Status** | Mandatory in India (Sec 135) | Always voluntary |
| **Strategic Intent** | Integrated with business strategy | Standalone charitable act |
| **Stakeholder Focus** | All stakeholders | Community / cause-specific |
| **Example** | Tata Steel investing in sustainable mining & community development (ongoing) | A company donating ₹10 lakh to a flood relief fund (one-time) |

**Key Difference:** CSR is **continuous, strategic, and integrated**; philanthropy is **discrete, reactive, and isolated**.

*(3 marks: 1 for clear difference + 1 for examples + 1 for table or summation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain **Carroll's Pyramid of CSR** in detail. State the significance of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **scope of CSR** with reference to internal, external, and global spheres. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q3(A)(a) — Carroll's Pyramid of CSR

**Introduction:**
Carroll's Pyramid of CSR, proposed by **Archie B. Carroll in 1991**, is one of the most widely accepted conceptual frameworks defining the nature and dimensions of corporate social responsibility.

**The Four Layers (Bottom to Top):**

**Layer 1 — Economic Responsibilities (Base):**
- The foundation of all business activity.
- Companies must be **profitable** to sustain themselves and provide value to society.
- Includes production of goods/services, employment generation, and fair pricing.
- *Example:* A company paying fair wages and earning reasonable profit.

**Layer 2 — Legal Responsibilities:**
- Businesses must operate **within the framework of law**.
- Includes compliance with labor laws, environmental regulations, taxation, and consumer protection.
- *Example:* Filing GST returns, following Factories Act, complying with pollution control norms.

**Layer 3 — Ethical Responsibilities:**
- Conducting business in a manner that is **fair, just, and morally right** — even when not legally required.
- Includes honesty in advertising, respecting employee dignity, fair supplier treatment.
- *Example:* A company voluntarily disclosing product risks even when not legally bound.

**Layer 4 — Philanthropic Responsibilities (Apex):**
- Voluntary contributions to community welfare.
- Includes donations, building schools, disaster relief, environmental conservation.
- *Example:* Infosys Foundation's education and rural development programs.

**Significance:**
- Provides a **holistic view** of a company's duties.
- Helps managers **prioritize** responsibilities.
- Acts as a **diagnostic tool** for CSR auditing.

---

### Model Solution to Q3(A)(b) — Scope of CSR

**Introduction:**
The scope of CSR extends across **three concentric spheres** of business influence.

**(i) Internal Sphere:**
- Concerns **employees, shareholders, and management** directly involved in the company.
- Activities: Health & safety, fair wages, training, work-life balance, profit-sharing, transparent governance.
- *Example:* Wipro's "Spirit of Wipro" that fosters employee engagement.

**(ii) External Sphere:**
- Concerns **customers, suppliers, local community, NGOs, and government**.
- Activities: Product quality, ethical sourcing, community development, rural programs.
- *Example:* Hindustan Unilever's "Project Shakti" empowering rural women entrepreneurs.

**(iii) Global Sphere:**
- Concerns **environment, future generations, and international norms**.
- Activities: Carbon reduction, biodiversity protection, human rights, UN SDG alignment.
- *Example:* Mahindra Group's commitment to carbon neutrality by 2040.

**Conclusion:**
The modern understanding of CSR requires firms to act responsibly across **all three spheres** — local and global — making CSR a holistic, strategic discipline.

---

### Alternative Question (Internal Choice)

**Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** "CSR is not just charity; it is a strategic business imperative." Critically evaluate this statement using **Carroll's model** and **stakeholder theory**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **statutory framework of CSR in India** under the **Companies Act 2013**. Discuss its **strengths and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q3(B)(a) — CSR as Strategic Business Imperative

**Argument 1 — Carroll's Model Justifies the Statement:**

Carroll's pyramid shows that CSR includes **economic and legal responsibilities**, which are inherently *strategic* — they ensure the firm's survival and license to operate. A firm that fails economically or legally *cannot* be socially responsible. Thus, CSR is integral to strategy, not a peripheral activity.

**Argument 2 — Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984):**

- **Freeman's Stakeholder Theory** holds that firms must create value for **all stakeholders** — employees, customers, suppliers, community — not just shareholders.
- Strategic CSR = **mutual value creation**: the firm profits *because* it serves society well.
- *Example:* Tesla's investment in electric vehicles serves the environmental stakeholder group *and* drives its premium brand valuation.

**Counter-Argument (Critical View):**

- Critics (e.g., **Milton Friedman**, 1970) argue that CSR **diverts resources** from the firm's primary economic purpose and reduces shareholder returns.
- Some CSR activities are "**greenwashing**" — image-building without substance.

**Synthesis:**

In the **21st-century stakeholder economy**, CSR is no longer optional charity. Companies like **Patagonia, Tata, Unilever** demonstrate that CSR and profitability are **complementary, not contradictory**. The statement is justified — with the caveat that CSR must be authentic, measurable, and integrated into strategy.

---

### Model Solution to Q3(B)(b) — Statutory Framework of CSR in India

**Legal Provision:**
**Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013** and the **Companies (Corporate Social Responsibility Policy) Rules, 2014** (amended in 2021) provide the statutory framework.

**Key Provisions:**

**(i) Applicability:**
A company must constitute a CSR Committee if it meets **any one** of the following criteria in the preceding financial year:
- Net worth ≥ ₹500 crore, **OR**
- Turnover ≥ ₹1,000 crore, **OR**
- Net profit ≥ ₹5 crore.

**(ii) CSR Committee:**
- Minimum **3 directors**, with at least **1 independent director**.
- Responsible for formulating the CSR Policy and monitoring expenditure.

**(iii) CSR Expenditure:**
- Companies must spend **at least 2% of the average net profits** made during the **3 immediately preceding financial years**.

**(iv) Eligible Activities:**
Activities listed in **Schedule VII**, including:
- Eradicating hunger and poverty
- Promoting education and gender equality
- Combating HIV/AIDS, malaria
- Environmental sustainability
- Protection of national heritage, art & culture
- Disaster management
- Rural development, slum area development

**(v) Reporting:**
- Annual filing of **CSR-2 Form** with the Registrar of Companies.
- Disclosure in the **Board's Report**.

**Strengths:**
- Converts voluntary CSR into a **legal obligation**.
- Brings **transparency** and accountability.
- Channels corporate funds toward **national priorities**.

**Limitations:**
- **Penal provisions are weak** — non-compliance attracts only fines, not imprisonment for non-executive directors.
- The "**comply-or-explain**" loophole allows companies to spend less by giving reasons.
- Focuses on **spending** rather than **impact**.
- Does not adequately cover **small and medium enterprises**.

**Conclusion:**
India's CSR law is a pioneering step, but its effectiveness depends on **stronger enforcement** and a shift from **output-based** to **outcome-based** evaluation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Nature of CSR" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CSR with Charity** — Many students write "CSR means donating money." This is *philanthropy*, not the full nature of CSR. You lose **2–3 marks** for this oversimplification.
> 2. **Skipping the Legal Dimension** — In Indian context, forgetting to mention **Section 135 and Schedule VII** is a guaranteed mark loss. Examiners *expect* statutory awareness.
> 3. **Listing without Explaining** — A list of 10 characteristics of CSR without explanation gets only **partial marks**. Each characteristic must be **justified with an example or reason**.
> 4. **No Diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners explicitly reward **Carroll's Pyramid diagram** or stakeholder maps. A well-labeled pyramid can fetch **2–3 bonus marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring Critics** — A 14-mark question asking you to "discuss" or "critically evaluate" CSR *demands* counter-arguments (Friedman, greenwashing, tokenism). One-sided answers cap at **8–9 marks maximum**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Nature of CSR"**

- ✅ **Definition**: CSR = business commitment to ethical conduct, economic development, and quality-of-life improvement for stakeholders and society.
- ✅ **Indian Mandate**: **Section 135 of Companies Act 2013** — spend **2% of 3-year average net profit**.
- ✅ **Eligibility**: Net worth ≥ ₹500 Cr **OR** Turnover ≥ ₹1,000 Cr **OR** Net Profit ≥ ₹5 Cr.
- ✅ **Carroll's Pyramid (1991)**: **E + L + Et + Ph** = Economic + Legal + Ethical + Philanthropic.
- ✅ **Three Spheres**: Internal (employees, owners), External (community, suppliers), Global (environment, future generations).
- ✅ **Schedule VII** areas: Education, health, environment, gender, rural development, disaster relief, heritage.
- ✅ **Reporting**: Annual filing of **CSR-2 Form** with MCA.
- ✅ **Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984)**: Duty to *all* affected parties, not just shareholders.
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994)**: **People + Planet + Profit**.
- ✅ **Key Distinction**: CSR ≠ Charity ≠ Philanthropy ≠ Sustainability (all are overlapping but distinct concepts).
- ✅ **Critical Lens**: Friedman's shareholder view vs. Freeman's stakeholder view — know both sides for 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Diagrams Matter**: Always include **Carroll's Pyramid** and/or **Stakeholder Map** for full marks in long-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSR Principles and Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT756 - BUSINESS ETHICS AND CORPORATE GOVERNANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CSR Principles and Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a company to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society — including economic, social, and environmental.

**Archie B. Carroll's Classical Definition (1979, 1991, 2016):**
> *"The social responsibility of business encompasses the economic, legal, ethical, and discretionary (philanthropic) expectations that society has of organizations at a given point in time."*

**World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD):**
> *"CSR is the continuing commitment by business to contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce and their families as well as of the local community and society at large."*

**European Commission Definition:**
> *"CSR is a concept whereby companies integrate social and environmental concerns in their business operations and in their interaction with their stakeholders on a voluntary basis."*

**Indian Statutory Definition (Section 135, Companies Act 2013):**
> *"CSR means the activities undertaken by a company in pursuance of its statutory obligation laid down in section 135(5), towards taking care of the concerns of the surrounding community and the environment."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Good Citizen of Society" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a company is a **citizen living in a neighborhood (society)**. Just as a responsible citizen is expected to:
>
> - **Earn a livelihood** and pay taxes → *Economic Responsibility*
> - **Obey the law** of the land → *Legal Responsibility*
> - **Do what is right and just**, even when not legally required → *Ethical Responsibility*
> - **Voluntarily help neighbors** in distress, donate to community causes → *Philanthropic Responsibility*
>
> A corporation, being a "legal person" operating within society, has parallel obligations. CSR is the company's way of being a **good corporate citizen** — it is the firm's contract with society.

A simpler intuition: **A business does not operate in a vacuum.** It draws resources (labor, raw materials, capital) from society, and society therefore expects it to give back. CSR formalizes this reciprocal relationship.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Benchmark (India, Section 135, Companies Act 2013):**
> Every company meeting the eligibility criteria must spend **at least 2% of its average net profits** made during the three immediately preceding financial years on CSR activities.
>
> **Minimum Applicability Criteria:**
> - Net worth ≥ **₹500 crore**, OR
> - Turnover ≥ **₹1,000 crore**, OR
> - Net Profit ≥ **₹5 crore**

**Other Standard CSR Metrics:**

| Metric | Description | Standard Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **CSR Spend Ratio** | CSR expenditure / Average Net Profit | ≥ 2% (India, statutory) |
| **ESG Score** | Environmental, Social, Governance composite rating | 0–100 scale |
| **GRI Reporting** | Global Reporting Initiative disclosures | Voluntary global standard |
| **B Corp Certification** | Certified B Corporation social/environmental score | ≥ 80 points |
| **SDGs Alignment** | UN Sustainable Development Goals mapping | 17 goals, 169 targets |

---

## 1.4 The "Why" of CSR — The Underlying Logic

The modern logic of CSR rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984):** A firm owes duties to *all* parties affected by its operations — not only shareholders.
2. **License to Operate:** Society can revoke a firm's social license through regulation, protest, or boycott if it misbehaves.
3. **Long-Term Value Creation:** Sustainability of profits requires sustainability of the ecosystem (social, environmental) that generates them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Carroll's CSR Pyramid — The Cornerstone Framework

Archie Carroll's (1991) pyramid organizes CSR into four hierarchical layers, arranged from **base to apex** in descending order of importance. Together, these form the **Total CSR Responsibility**.

$$
\text{Total CSR} = C_{\text{Economic}} + C_{\text{Legal}} + C_{\text{Ethical}} + C_{\text{Philanthropic}}
$$

Where $C_{\text{component}}$ represents the contribution of each responsibility layer.

> [!NOTE]
> The pyramid does **not** mean one layer is more "important" than another in absolute terms — it represents the **frequency of occurrence** in business life. Economic responsibilities form the base because they are the most fundamental and constant.

### Layer 1 — Economic Responsibilities (The Foundation)
- **Definition:** The obligation to produce goods/services society needs and sell them at a profit.
- **Core Question:** *Will the business be profitable?*
- **Stakeholders:** Shareholders, employees, customers, suppliers.
- **Examples:** Sound operations, competitive pricing, job creation, dividend payments.

### Layer 2 — Legal Responsibilities (The Second Tier)
- **Definition:** The duty to operate within the legal and regulatory framework of the country.
- **Core Question:** *Is the business playing by the rules?*
- **Stakeholders:** Government, regulators, judiciary.
- **Examples:** Tax compliance, labor law adherence, environmental regulations, consumer protection laws.

### Layer 3 — Ethical Responsibilities (The Third Tier)
- **Definition:** Going beyond legal compliance to do what is *right, just, and fair*, even when not legally mandated.
- **Core Question:** *Is the business doing what is expected by society beyond the law?*
- **Stakeholders:** Community, civil society, employees, customers.
- **Examples:** Fair treatment of workers beyond statutory minimum, transparency, avoiding exploitative practices, respecting cultural norms.

### Layer 4 — Philanthropic Responsibilities (The Apex)
- **Definition:** Voluntary contributions to community welfare, often termed **corporate citizenship**.
- **Core Question:** *Is the business a good corporate citizen contributing to the quality of life?*
- **Stakeholders:** Community, NGOs, society at large.
- **Examples:** Charitable donations, building schools/hospitals, disaster relief, employee volunteering programs.

---

## 2.2 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — The "3P" Framework

Coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, TBL expands the traditional single bottom line (Profit) to three interlinked dimensions:

$$
\text{TBL Performance} = f(\text{People}, \text{Planet}, \text{Profit})
$$

| Dimension | Focus | Sample Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| **People (Social)** | Human capital, community welfare, labor practices | Employee satisfaction index, gender pay ratio, community grievance count |
| **Planet (Environmental)** | Ecological footprint, resource use, emissions | Carbon footprint (tCO₂e), water usage, waste recycled % |
| **Profit (Economic)** | Financial returns, shareholder value | ROE, ROA, Economic Value Added (EVA) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> True sustainability is achieved only when a firm performs well on **all three** dimensions simultaneously. Sacrificing one for the other is *not* genuine TBL.

---

## 2.3 Drivers of CSR — Why Companies Adopt CSR

CSR drivers are categorized into **Internal Drivers** (firm-controlled) and **External Drivers** (environment-controlled).

| Category | Driver | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Ethical leadership and values | Voluntary, Value-driven |
| **Internal** | Cost & risk reduction | Strategic |
| **Internal** | Employee attraction & retention | Strategic, HR-led |
| **Internal** | Innovation & new market opportunities | Strategic |
| **Internal** | Brand equity & reputation | Strategic |
| **External** | Government regulation & legal mandates | Coercive |
| **External** | Investor/Shareholder pressure | Stakeholder-driven |
| **External** | Customer demand & ethical consumerism | Market-driven |
| **External** | Media scrutiny & public activism | Social pressure |
| **External** | NGO & civil society pressure | Advocacy-driven |
| **External** | Competitive pressure | Strategic |
| **External** | International standards (UN SDGs, GRI, ISO 26000) | Normative |

---

## 2.4 Strategies of CSR

CSR is not a one-size-fits-all activity. The classical typology of CSR strategies (Porter & Kramer, **2006, HBR**) distinguishes between:

### 2.4.1 Strategic CSR (Creating Shared Value — CSV)
- Aligns CSR with **core business strategy and profit-generation**.
- Seeks competitive advantage through social/environmental investments.
- Win-win logic: society's problems = business opportunities.
- **Example:** Tata's low-cost water purifier (Swachh) — addresses waterborne disease + opens rural market.

### 2.4.2 Responsive CSR
- Responds to the **social, environmental, and stakeholder concerns** of the day.
- Defensive/Adaptive in nature: mitigating negative impacts.
- Aligns with the existing business model.
- **Example:** Adopting cleaner technology to reduce emissions in response to environmental regulations.

### 2.4.3 Defensive CSR (or "Strategic Philanthropy")
- Engages in CSR primarily to **protect reputation and deflect criticism**.
- Often criticized as window-dressing or "greenwashing."

> [!TIP]
> **CSV vs CSR — Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> $$\underbrace{\text{CSR}}_{\text{Discretionary, often outside core business}} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{CSV}}_{\text{Embedded in business strategy, drives competitive advantage}}$$

---

## 2.5 CSR Models — Comparative Theoretical Perspectives

| Model | Theorist/School | Core Philosophy | View of Profit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classical / Economic Model** | Milton Friedman (1970) | Business has one responsibility: maximize profit within rules of the game. | **Primary objective** |
| **Socio-Economic Model** | Keith Davis, Archie Carroll | Business has broader social obligations beyond profit. | **Means to social ends** |
| **Socio-Legal Model** | Legal scholars | Business must comply with both law and social expectations. | **Constrained by law & society** |
| **Stakeholder Model** | R. Edward Freeman (1984) | Business owes duties to all stakeholders. | **One of many legitimate interests** |
| **Triple Bottom Line** | John Elkington (1994) | Profit, People, Planet are co-equal. | **One of three bottom lines** |
| **Creating Shared Value (CSV)** | Porter & Kramer (2011) | Social problems = economic opportunities. | **Re-defined by social value** |
| **Sustainable Development Model** | Brundtland Commission (1987) | Development that meets present needs without compromising future generations. | **Long-term, intergenerational** |

---

## 2.6 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Formulas, Ratios & Boundaries

| Concept | Notation / Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carroll's Total CSR | $T_{CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph$ | Sum of all four responsibilities | Strategic self-audit |
| TBL Index | $I_{TBL} = \dfrac{P_{econ} + P_{soc} + P_{env}}{3}$ | Composite sustainability score | Cross-firm comparison |
| Statutory CSR Spend | $S_{CSR} \geq 0.02 \times \overline{NP}_{3yr}$ | 2% of 3-year avg. net profit | India, Section 135 |
| Carbon Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ (tonnes)}}{\text{Revenue (₹ Cr)}}$ | Emissions efficiency | Environmental KPI |
| Stakeholder Salience | Power, Legitimacy, Urgency (Mitchell, Agle, Wood) | Priority for attention | Stakeholder mapping |
| Social Return on Investment | $SROI = \dfrac{\text{Social Value Created (₹)}}{\text{CSR Investment (₹)}}$ | Social impact per rupee spent | Impact evaluation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse Carroll's "philanthropic" with "ethical" responsibilities. Remember: *ethical = doing what is right*; *philanthropic = voluntary giving beyond expectations*. The line is **statutory compliance vs voluntary contribution**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of CSR Frameworks

| Framework | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| Carroll's Pyramid | Diagnostic self-audit; gap analysis of CSR activities |
| TBL | Sustainability reporting (annual reports, BRSR) |
| Drivers Analysis | Stakeholder engagement strategy |
| CSV | Strategic integration of CSR into business model |
| Stakeholder Model | Materiality assessment; risk mapping |
| SDGs | International reporting & global alignment |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is a management/ethics subject. "Derivations" here mean **conceptual construction** of frameworks, comparison matrices, and analytical logic — not mathematical theorems. The rigor lies in the *step-by-step logic* of how each framework is built and applied.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: How Carroll's Pyramid Was Constructed

**Step 1 — Identify Business Functions**
Carroll began by asking: *"What does society expect of business in totality?"* He listed the major categories of expectations a business must fulfill in any modern economy.

**Step 2 — Classify by Type of Expectation**
He grouped expectations into four mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive (MECE) categories:
- Expectations about **economic output** → Economic responsibilities
- Expectations about **legal conduct** → Legal responsibilities
- Expectations about **moral conduct** → Ethical responsibilities
- Expectations about **voluntary good deeds** → Philanthropic responsibilities

**Step 3 — Rank by Frequency & Fundamental Nature**
He placed **economic** at the base (most fundamental, constant), and **philanthropic** at the apex (least expected, discretionary):

$$
\text{Layer Order (base to apex)} = \{E, L, Et, Ph\}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a Hierarchical Model**
Visualize as a pyramid; the total CSR contribution of a firm becomes:

$$
C_{Total} = w_E \cdot C_E + w_L \cdot C_L + w_{Et} \cdot C_{Et} + w_{Ph} \cdot C_{Ph}
$$

Where each weight $w$ reflects the relative emphasis society places on that responsibility.

**Step 5 — Apply to Decision-Making**
A manager can use this model to triage CSR questions:
1. *Is it profitable?* → Economic check ✓
2. *Is it legal?* → Legal check ✓
3. *Is it right?* → Ethical check ✓
4. *Is it good for community?* → Philanthropic check ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The CSR Strategy Formulation (5-Stage Logical Flow)

**Stage 1 — Stakeholder Identification**
Enumerate *all* stakeholders (internal + external) affected by firm operations.

$$
S_{firmed} = \{ \text{Shareholders, Employees, Customers, Suppliers, Community, Govt, NGOs, Media, Environment} \}
$$

**Stage 2 — Materiality Assessment**
Identify the *most important* CSR issues for the firm, typically using a **double-materiality matrix**:
- *Financial materiality:* impact on enterprise value
- *Impact materiality:* impact on society/environment

**Stage 3 — Strategic Alignment**
Map selected CSR themes onto the firm's **core competencies** to ensure strategic fit (CSV logic):

$$
\text{Strategic Fit} = f(\text{Firm Core Competency}, \text{Social Need})
$$

**Stage 4 — Implementation Design**
Choose governance mode:
- Direct in-house programs
- Partnerships with NGOs
- CSR foundations
- Multi-stakeholder platforms

**Stage 5 — Measurement, Reporting & Iteration**
Establish KPIs, align with **BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report)** in India, GRI globally, and SDGs where applicable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing CSR Strategies

| Strategy Type | Driver Logic | Profit Linkage | Sustainability Risk | KTU-Evaluated Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic CSR / CSV** | Long-term competitive advantage | **High — built-in** | Low | ★★★★★ |
| **Responsive CSR** | Compliance + reputation | Medium | Medium | ★★★☆☆ |
| **Defensive CSR** | Reputation protection | Low–Medium | High (exposed to scrutiny) | ★★☆☆☆ |
| **Philanthropic CSR** | Pure altruism | Very Low | Low | ★★★☆☆ |
| **Greenwashing** | Deception | None | **Very High** | ☆☆☆☆☆ |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Indian CSR Spend Areas (Schedule VII, Companies Act 2013)

| Schedule VII Area | Linkage to SDGs | Typical CSR Project | Example Company |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eradicating hunger, poverty | SDG 1, 2 | Mid-day meals, food banks | Akshaya Patra (ISKCON) |
| Promoting education | SDG 4 | Schools, scholarships, vocational training | Infosys Foundation |
| Gender equality & empowerment | SDG 5 | Skill training for women | Mahindra Group |
| Environmental sustainability | SDG 13, 14, 15 | Afforestation, water conservation | Welspun |
| Rural development | SDG 11 | Sanitation, rural infra | Tata Trusts |
| Health & sanitation | SDG 3, 6 | Mobile health units, Swachh Bharat | Reliance Foundation |

---

## 3.5 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right CSR Approach

$$
\text{Is the social issue linked to your core business?}
\begin{cases}
\text{YES} \longrightarrow \text{Adopt CSV approach (Strategic CSR)} \\[4pt]
\text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{Is it required by law?} 
\begin{cases}
\text{YES} \longrightarrow \text{Responsive CSR} \\[4pt]
\text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{Is it ethically expected?} 
\begin{cases}
\text{YES} \longrightarrow \text{Ethical CSR} \\[4pt]
\text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{Philanthropic CSR}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Analytical Example: "CSR Audit" of a Hypothetical Firm

**Firm X** — A textile manufacturer
- Spends on education in villages (Philanthropic ✓)
- Pays minimum wages only (Legal ✓, but Ethical ✗)
- Pollutes a local river (Legal ✗ if regulation exists)
- Has 0% renewable energy use (Environmental ✗)

**CSR Score (analytical reasoning):**
- Economic layer: ✓✓✓ (Profitable)
- Legal layer: Partial — pollution violations = Legal ✗
- Ethical layer: ✗ — Minimum wage only, no welfare measures
- Philanthropic layer: ✓ — Education programs ongoing

**Diagnosis:** Firm X is operating in a **"Philanthropic Shell"** — looks good externally but has serious ethical and legal gaps. It must focus on **Carroll's lower two layers first** before scaling up philanthropy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Carroll's CSR Pyramid — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["PHILANTHROPIC RESPONSIBILITY\nCorporate Citizenship\nDonations, Volunteering, Community Programs"]:::apex
    L3["ETHICAL RESPONSIBILITY\nDo what is right, just and fair\nBeyond legal compliance"]:::tier3
    L2["LEGAL RESPONSIBILITY\nObey the law of the land\nTax, labor, environmental laws"]:::tier2
    L1["ECONOMIC RESPONSIBILITY\nProduce goods, services at a profit\nBase of the pyramid"]:::base

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    classDef base fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef tier3 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef apex fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.2 Triple Bottom Line (3P) — Concentric Impact Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PROFIT\nEconomic\nPerformance\nRevenue, ROI, EVA"]:::profit
    B["PEOPLE\nSocial\nCapital\nEmployees, Community,\nStakeholder Welfare"]:::people
    C["PLANET\nEnvironmental\nStewardship\nCarbon, Water, Biodiversity,\nWaste Management"]:::planet

    A --- B
    B --- C
    C --- A

    D["SUSTAINABILITY\nAT THE INTERSECTION\nOF ALL THREE"]:::center

    classDef profit fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef people fill:#ffe699,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef planet fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef center fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 CSR Strategy Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Identify Social Issue]) --> Q1{Linked to Core Business?}
    Q1 -- YES --> CSV["CSV APPROACH\nStrategic CSR\nLong-term Win-Win"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Required by Law?}
    Q2 -- YES --> Resp["RESPONSIVE CSR\nCompliance + Reputation\nAdaptive Measures"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Ethically Expected?}
    Q3 -- YES --> Eth["ETHICAL CSR\nBeyond Compliance\nFairness, Transparency"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Phil["PHILANTHROPIC CSR\nVoluntary Giving\nCharity, Donations"]
    CSV --> Out([CSR Impact Maximized])
    Resp --> Out
    Eth --> Out
    Phil --> Out

    classDef startend fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    classDef csvs fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    classDef phile fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000

    class Start,Out startend
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class CSV,Resp csvs
    class Eth,Phil phile
```

---

## 4.4 Stakeholder Salience Model (Mitchell, Agle & Wood, 1997)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Salience["STAKEHOLDER SALIENCE MODEL"]
        direction TB
        Def["DEFINITIVE\n(Power + Legitimacy + Urgency)\nPRIMARY FOCUS"]:::def
        Dom["DOMINANT\n(Power + Legitimacy)\nKeep Satisfied"]:::dom
        Dang["DANGEROUS\n(Power + Urgency)\nManage Carefully"]:::dang
        Dep["DEPENDENT\n(Legitimacy + Urgency)\nKeep Informed"]:::dep
```
