```mermaid
        Lat["LATENT\nPower OR Legitimacy OR Urgency\nMonitor"]:::lat
        Non["NON-STAKEHOLDER\nNone of the three\nLowest Priority"]:::non
    end

    classDef def fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef dom fill:#9bc2e6,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef dang fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef dep fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef lat fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef non fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.5 CSR Implementation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Board of Directors\nCSR Committee\n(Section 135)"]
        A2["CSR Policy\n(Statutory + Voluntary)"]
        A3["Budget Allocation\n2% of Avg. Net Profit"]
    end

    subgraph B["PROCESS LAYER"]
        B1["Project Identification\nNeed Assessment"]
        B2["Partner Selection\nNGO / Foundation / Govt"]
        B3["Execution & Monitoring\nKPI Tracking"]
    end

    subgraph C["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Direct Beneficiaries\n(Lives impacted)"]
        C2["Sustainability Outcomes\n(SDG Indicators)"]
        C3["Disclosures\n(BRSR, Annual Report)"]
    end

    subgraph D["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        D1["Impact Assessment\n(SROI, Audits)"]
        D2["Stakeholder Engagement"]
        D3["Strategy Refinement"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 -.-> A2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> Common mark-loss areas in CSR questions:
> 1. Confusing **Ethical** with **Philanthropic** responsibilities (Carroll's Pyramid) — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. Writing CSR as "charity only" — failing to mention **strategic / CSV** dimension costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 3. Forgetting **statutory provisions** of Section 135, Companies Act 2013 (2% rule, applicability criteria) — a common 2-mark loss.
> 4. In strategy questions, students list drivers but **fail to classify** them as internal vs external — examiners explicitly ask this.
> 5. Writing **Triple Bottom Line** as "People, Planet, Product" (wrong!) — correct is **People, Planet, Profit**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define CSR. Explain any two principles of CSR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Corporate Social Responsibility is the continuing commitment of business to contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce, their families, the local community, and society at large (WBCSD). In India, Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013 makes CSR spending mandatory for eligible companies.

**Two Key Principles:**

1. **Principle of Accountability:** A firm must be accountable not just to shareholders but to *all* stakeholders — employees, customers, community, environment, and government. Modern corporations are "legal persons" embedded in society and must answer for their impacts.

2. **Principle of Sustainability:** CSR must be guided by the goal of sustainable development — meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs. This anchors CSR in long-term thinking rather than short-term optics.

*(Alternatively, students may state — Principle of Transparency, Principle of Ethical Conduct, Principle of Stakeholder Inclusiveness, Principle of Triple Bottom Line.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is meant by Triple Bottom Line? Briefly explain its three components.**

**Model Answer:**

Triple Bottom Line (TBL), coined by **John Elkington in 1994**, is a sustainability framework that measures a firm's performance against three interlinked dimensions instead of the traditional single bottom line of profit.

The three components are:

1. **People (Social):** Refers to the firm's impact on human capital and social well-being — fair labor practices, employee welfare, community development, and human rights.
2. **Planet (Environmental):** Refers to ecological stewardship — reducing carbon emissions, conserving water, managing waste, and protecting biodiversity.
3. **Profit (Economic):** Refers to financial performance — revenue, profitability, shareholder returns, and economic value creation.

A firm achieves **true sustainability** only when it performs well on **all three** dimensions simultaneously. TBL has become the global standard for integrated reporting (e.g., GRI, BRSR in India).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Under KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **ONE** full question from each module (typically with internal choice Q1 OR Q2). Below are the two alternative question choices, each with 7+7 sub-parts.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q(A). [KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Question]**
**(a)** Explain in detail **Archie Carroll's CSR Pyramid** with suitable examples. Discuss its relevance in the Indian corporate context. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the various **strategies of CSR** that firms can adopt. How is **Creating Shared Value (CSV)** different from traditional CSR? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Archie Carroll's (1979, 1991) CSR Pyramid is the most widely cited framework for understanding corporate social responsibility. It argues that society expects firms to fulfill four categories of responsibilities simultaneously.

**The Four Layers (4 marks):**

| Layer | Responsibility | Description | Example (Indian Context) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Base) | **Economic** | Produce goods/services profitably | TCS delivering IT services, generating ₹₹ |
| 2 | **Legal** | Obey laws and regulations | Tata Steel complying with environmental norms |
| 3 | **Ethical** | Do what is right beyond law | Infosys' open-door policy, employee dignity |
| 4 (Apex) | **Philanthropic** | Voluntary contributions to society | Tata Trusts — 66% of profit goes to charity |

**Logical Relationship (1 mark):**
The pyramid suggests that all four layers together form the *Total CSR* of a firm:

$$
T_{CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph
$$

A firm lacking any one layer is incomplete in its social contract.

**Indian Context (1 mark):**
Carroll's pyramid directly maps onto India's **Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013**:
- *Economic* → Profitability threshold for CSR applicability
- *Legal* → Statutory 2% spend on CSR
- *Ethical* → SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report)
- *Philanthropic* → Schedule VII activities (education, health, environment)

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Carroll's pyramid remains a foundational tool for diagnosing CSR health, though modern extensions integrate **stakeholder theory** and **CSV**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
CSR strategies are the operational approaches firms adopt to discharge their social responsibilities. The classical typology (Porter & Kramer, 2006) classifies them into three main types: **Strategic, Responsive, and Defensive**.

**Strategy 1 — Strategic CSR / Creating Shared Value (CSV) (2 marks):**
- Embeds social/environmental goals **into** the business model.
- Win-win logic: addressing social problems creates economic value.
- **Example:** Hindustan Unilever's "Project Shakti" — employs rural women as micro-entrepreneurs (social empowerment + market expansion).

**Strategy 2 — Responsive CSR (1.5 marks):**
- Responds to the concerns of stakeholders, society, and environment.
- Defensive/Adaptive — does not create new value, but mitigates harm.
- **Example:** Automobile firms adopting BS-VI emission norms ahead of regulatory deadlines.

**Strategy 3 — Defensive CSR (1 mark):**
- Primarily aimed at **reputation management** and avoiding criticism.
- Often criticized as **greenwashing** if not backed by real action.
- **Example:** One-off token donations during crisis periods.

**CSV vs Traditional CSR — Key Differences (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Traditional CSR | CSV |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Give back to society | Address social issues through business |
| Profit link | Often separate | **Integral** to profit model |
| Mindset | Discretionary, charitable | Strategic, core to operations |
| Stakeholders | Community (peripheral) | **All** stakeholders |
| Measurement | CSR spend (2%) | **Shared value created** |
| Time horizon | Short to medium | **Long-term** |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Modern firms increasingly pivot from traditional philanthropy to **CSV**, aligning social impact with competitive advantage.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q(B). [KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Question]**
**(a)** Explain the **drivers of CSR** in detail, classifying them into internal and external categories. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Critically analyze the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** approach. Discuss its strengths and limitations with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
CSR drivers are the forces — internal or external to a firm — that motivate or compel it to adopt socially responsible practices. Understanding them is essential for designing an effective CSR strategy.

**Internal Drivers (3 marks):**

| Driver | Explanation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ethical leadership** | Personal values of top management | Ratan Tata's leadership at Tata Group |
| **Cost & risk reduction** | CSR reduces long-term operational risks | Adopting energy-efficient processes |
| **Employee engagement** | CSR boosts morale, retention, and recruitment | Google.org's employee volunteering |
| **Innovation** | Solving social problems opens new markets | Aravind Eye Hospital's low-cost model |
| **Reputation & brand** | CSR enhances brand equity | Patagonia's environmental brand |
| **Investor pressure** | ESG-aware investors | BlackRock's ESG mandates |

**External Drivers (3 marks):**

| Driver | Explanation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Government regulation** | Statutory mandates | Section 135, Companies Act 2013 |
| **Customer demand** | Ethical consumerism | Demand for organic / fair-trade products |
| **NGO & civil society** | Advocacy and pressure | Greenpeace vs. oil companies |
| **Media scrutiny** | Public exposure | Social media campaigns |
| **International standards** | Global norms | UN SDGs, GRI, ISO 26000 |
| **Competition** | Industry-level adoption | CSR as a competitive differentiator |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
A balanced CSR strategy responds to **both** internal motivations and external pressures, achieving authenticity and resilience.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, coined by John Elkington (1994), proposes that firms should measure performance across three dimensions: **People (Social), Planet (Environmental), and Profit (Economic)**. It is often summarized as **3P**.

**Strengths of TBL (3 marks):**

1. **Holistic view of performance** — moves beyond profit-only metrics.
2. **Aligns with sustainability** — encourages long-term, intergenerational thinking.
3. **Supports integrated reporting** — adopted by GRI, BRSR, IFRS S1/S2.
4. **Stakeholder inclusivity** — accounts for the welfare of all parties, not only shareholders.
5. **Drives innovation** — pushes firms to find new solutions that serve multiple bottom lines.

**Limitations of TBL (3 marks):**

1. **Measurement difficulty** — quantifying social and environmental impact is challenging (e.g., assigning a monetary value to biodiversity).
2. **Trade-off problem** — sometimes the 3Ps conflict; firms face dilemmas (e.g., cheap energy vs. environment).
3. **Trade-offs across time** — short-term economic pain may be required for long-term social/environmental gain.
4. **Reporting bias** — firms may "cherry-pick" favorable metrics (the TBL paradox).
5. **Lack of universal standards** — different firms use different 3P metrics, making comparison hard.

**Examples (0.5 mark):**
- **Positive:** Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan — improved livelihoods of 800M+ while growing sustainably.
- **Negative:** Volkswagen's Dieselgate — claimed eco-friendly TBL but actually cheated emissions tests.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
TBL is a **powerful conceptual framework** but needs rigorous measurement standards and independent verification to deliver genuine sustainability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density, Exam-Ready)**

- [ ] **CSR Definition (Carroll, WBCSD, EC, Section 135)** — know all four formulations.
- [ ] **Carroll's Pyramid — 4 layers in order: Economic → Legal → Ethical → Philanthropic.**
- [ ] **TBL = People + Planet + Profit (3P) — NOT "Product."**
- [ ] **Section 135, Companies Act 2013 — 2% of 3-year avg. net profit; applicability criteria: ₹500 Cr net worth OR ₹1,000 Cr turnover OR ₹5 Cr profit.**
- [ ] **Carroll's Pyramid ≠ "Philanthropic is the most important"** — economic is the base because it's most fundamental.
- [ ] **Ethical = doing what is right, beyond law**; **Philanthropic = voluntary giving beyond expectations.**
- [ ] **CSV (Porter & Kramer, 2011)** — strategic, integrated with business, win-win.
- [ ] **CSR drivers — classify as Internal vs External** (board exams test this!).
- [ ] **Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984)** — duties to ALL parties, not only shareholders.
- [ ] **Indian CSR Schedule VII — 14 areas** (education, health, gender, environment, rural dev, etc.).
- [ ] **Friedman (1970) view** — business's job is profit maximization within legal/ethical bounds.
- [ ] **BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report)** — mandatory top-1000 listed firms in India (SEBI 2021+).
- [ ] **UN SDGs (17 goals, 169 targets)** — global alignment framework for CSR.
- [ ] **Strategic vs Responsive vs Defensive CSR** — three classical strategies.
- [ ] **Greenwashing** — false/cosmetic CSR claims; a serious ethical pitfall.
- [ ] **SROI = Social Value Created ÷ CSR Investment** — used for impact evaluation.
- [ ] **Mitchell, Agle & Wood's Stakeholder Salience Model** — Power + Legitimacy + Urgency.
- [ ] **Brundtland Report (1987)** — origin of the modern sustainability definition.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Carroll's Pyramid — "ELEPHANT"**
>
> **E**conomic → **L**egal → **E**thical → **P**hilanthropic → **H**igher in pyramid → **A**pex tier → **N**ot just charity → **T**otal CSR
>
> *ELE-PH-ANT* → base to apex: **E → L → E → P**
>
> **TBL Mnemonic — "3 Ps": People, Planet, Profit.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models and Best Practices of CSR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT756 - BUSINESS ETHICS AND CORPORATE GOVERNANCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Models and Best Practices of CSR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) Models** are systematic, structured frameworks that conceptualize, classify, and operationalize a firm's social, environmental, and economic obligations towards its stakeholders. These models translate the abstract philosophy of CSR into actionable managerial pillars, measurable outcomes, and strategic decision-making blueprints.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT756 – Module 4)**, CSR models are studied under three principal lenses:

1. **Hierarchical / Pyramid Models** – exemplified by **Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991)**.
2. **Performance-Based Models** – exemplified by **Elkington's Triple Bottom Line (1994)**.
3. **Relational / Stakeholder Models** – rooted in **R. Edward Freeman's Stakeholder Theory (1984)**.

**Best Practices of CSR** refer to the empirically proven, repeatable, and scalable CSR strategies adopted by globally and nationally acclaimed corporations that serve as benchmarks for ethical, sustainable, and stakeholder-centric business conduct.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Good Citizen" Analogy** 🏘️
> 
> Imagine a person living in a housing colony. To be considered a *good citizen*, they are expected to:
> 1. **Pay their bills and taxes** (Economic responsibility)
> 2. **Follow society's rules and bylaws** (Legal responsibility)
> 3. **Behave ethically – don't cheat, don't litter** (Ethical responsibility)
> 4. **Volunteer at the community park on weekends** (Philanthropic responsibility)
> 
> A corporation, like a citizen, operates inside a "society" of customers, employees, governments, and the environment. **CSR Models are simply the rulebooks that define what it means to be a good corporate citizen**, while **Best Practices** are the inspiring stories of citizens (companies) who went above and beyond.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **CSR** | Corporate Social Responsibility | % of profit allocated (India: **2% under Sec. 135 of Companies Act 2013**) |
| **TBL** | Triple Bottom Line | Three P's: **People, Planet, Profit** |
| **ESG** | Environmental, Social, Governance | Composite score (0–100) by rating agencies |
| **SDG** | UN Sustainable Development Goals | **17 Goals, 169 Targets** (adopted 2015) |
| **Philanthropy** | Voluntary charitable giving | CSR spend / Net Profit ratio |
| **Sustainability** | Meeting present needs without compromising future generations | Carbon footprint, Water footprint, GRI Standards |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 expects students to *enumerate* at least 3 CSR models, *differentiate* between Philanthropy and Strategic CSR, and provide *at least 2 Indian + 2 Global best practice case studies* in any 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Model 1 — Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991)

**Archie B. Carroll** proposed that total corporate社会责任 comprises **four layered responsibilities**, arranged hierarchically like a pyramid. The foundation is the most basic expectation; the apex is the most aspirational.

### Layer-Wise Logical Breakdown:

- **Level 1 — Economic Responsibilities** *(Foundation)*
  - **Why:** A business exists first to produce goods/services profitably.
  - **How:** Maximize shareholder value, ensure operational viability, offer competitive pricing, generate employment.
  - **Examples:** Profitable operations, cost efficiency, ROI generation.

- **Level 2 — Legal Responsibilities** *(Compliance Layer)*
  - **Why:** Society codifies minimum acceptable conduct into laws.
  - **How:** Obey labour laws, environmental regulations, tax statutes, consumer protection acts.
  - **Examples:** GST compliance, Factories Act 1948, Pollution Control Board norms.

- **Level 3 — Ethical Responsibilities** *(Moral Layer)*
  - **Why:** Laws are minimums; society expects conduct *beyond* mere compliance.
  - **How:** Recognize unwritten norms, fairness, transparency, even when legally unrequired.
  - **Examples:** Fair treatment of suppliers, honest advertising, gender pay equity.

- **Level 4 — Philanthropic Responsibilities** *(Apex – Aspirational)*
  - **Why:** Corporations are expected to actively contribute to community welfare.
  - **How:** Voluntary donations, employee volunteering, building schools, disaster relief.
  - **Examples:** Tata Trusts' cancer hospitals, Infosys Foundation's education programs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Carroll, 1991):** The pyramid's shape is deliberate. Economic responsibilities form the *broadest base* because they are foundational. Philanthropic responsibilities are at the *narrow apex* because, while valuable, they are discretionary and not expected of every firm equally.

**Carroll's formula (conceptual):**

$$
\text{Total CSR} = E + L + Et + Ph
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Economic obligations
- $L$ = Legal obligations
- $Et$ = Ethical obligations
- $Ph$ = Philanthropic obligations

## 2.2 Model 2 — Triple Bottom Line (TBL) by John Elkington (1994)

Elkington argued that corporate performance must be measured against **three bottom lines** simultaneously, not just profit.

$$
\text{Corporate Performance} = \underbrace{\text{People}}_{\text{Social}} + \underbrace{\text{Planet}}_{\text{Environmental}} + \underbrace{\text{Profit}}_{\text{Economic}}
$$

### The Three Pillars:

- **People (Social Equity):** Fair wages, diversity & inclusion, human rights, community development.
- **Planet (Environmental Integrity):** Carbon neutrality, circular economy, biodiversity conservation, waste reduction.
- **Profit (Economic Prosperity):** Sustainable financial returns that fund the other two pillars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favourite:** A common 7-mark sub-question asks: *"Explain Triple Bottom Line with an example."* Always remember — TBL is *also known as 3Ps* and was published in *California Management Review (1994)*.

## 2.3 Model 3 — Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984)

According to **R. Edward Freeman**, a firm owes responsibility to **all parties affected by its operations**, not just shareholders. The theory maps **stakeholders** and their legitimate **claims**.

### Stakeholder Classification:

| Stakeholder Type | Examples | Primary Claim |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal – Primary** | Employees, Shareholders, Management | Wages, Dividends, Job Security |
| **External – Primary** | Customers, Suppliers, Lenders | Product Quality, Timely Payment |
| **External – Secondary** | Community, NGOs, Media, Government | Environmental Care, Legal Compliance |
| **Future Generations** | Society at large | Sustainability, Climate Action |

**Freeman's Stakeholder Map (Mitchell's Salience Model — refined by Mitchell, Agle & Wood, 1997)** classifies stakeholders by **Power, Legitimacy, and Urgency** into seven categories: Dormant, Discretionary, Demanding, Dominant, Dependent, Dangerous, and Definitive.

## 2.4 Model 4 — Corporate Citizenship Model

This model views the corporation as a **"citizen" of the country/community** where it operates, with full rights (legal personhood) and duties (social contribution). Variants include:

- **Local Citizenship:** Focus on the immediate community.
- **National Citizenship:** Compliance with national development goals.
- **Transnational/Global Citizenship:** Adherence to international norms (e.g., UN Global Compact, OECD Guidelines).

## 2.5 Model 5 — Sustainable Development Model (Brundtland, 1987)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (WCED, 1987):** *"Sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

This model integrates **economic growth, social inclusion, and environmental protection** as a single integrated agenda — operationalized today through the **UN's 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Models at a Glance

| # | Model | Proponent & Year | Core Idea | KTU-Mandated Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CSR Pyramid** | Archie B. Carroll, 1991 | 4 hierarchical layers: Economic, Legal, Ethical, Philanthropic | *"From base to apex"* |
| 2 | **Triple Bottom Line** | John Elkington, 1994 | 3 Ps: People, Planet, Profit | *"3 Ps / Sustainability"* |
| 3 | **Stakeholder Theory** | R. Edward Freeman, 1984 | Responsibility to all affected parties | *"Stakeholder Salience"* |
| 4 | **Corporate Citizenship** | Various, 1990s | Firm as a citizen with rights & duties | *"Local–Global Citizenship"* |
| 5 | **Sustainable Development** | WCED / Brundtland, 1987 | Inter-generational equity | *"Future generations"* |

## 2.7 Best Practices of CSR — Categorical Framework

Best practices can be classified into **three tiers** based on strategic depth:

### Tier 1 — Strategic / Embedded CSR
CSR is integrated into the **core business model** and value chain.
- **ITC Limited (India):** *e-Choupal* — digitalizes rural agri-supply chain, benefiting 4 million farmers.
- **Unilever (Global):** *Sustainable Living Plan* — committed to halving environmental footprint while doubling revenues.

### Tier 2 — Programmatic / Thematic CSR
CSR runs as a **flagship program** addressing a specific social issue.
- **Infosys Foundation (India):** *Akshara Patra* mid-day meals, rural libraries, disaster relief.
- **Google (Global):** *RE\<C* and *Google.org Impact Challenge* — renewable energy & AI for social good.

### Tier 3 — Philanthropic / Reactive CSR
CSR is **event-driven or donation-based**.
- **Tata Group (India):** 66% of Tata Sons' profits routed to Tata Trusts — philanthropy legacy.
- **Patagonia (Global):** 1% of sales to environmental NGOs; *"Don't Buy This Jacket"* campaign.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of CSR Models

- **Strategic Decision-Making:** TBL helps CFOs measure non-financial KPIs.
- **Reporting & Compliance:** Stakeholder Theory drives **GRI Standards** and **Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report (BRSR)** in India.
- **Investor Attraction:** ESG (rooted in TBL + Stakeholder) attracts **USD 30+ trillion** in sustainable investments globally.
- **Brand Equity:** Consumers increasingly prefer purpose-driven brands (e.g., **Patagonia, Tata**).
- **Risk Mitigation:** Ethical Layer of Carroll's Pyramid reduces legal penalties and reputational damage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Model Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Five Models

The following **3 × 3 comparative analysis matrix** is the *highest-yield content* for KTU 14-mark questions asking *"Compare and contrast CSR models"*.

| Comparison Parameter | Carroll's Pyramid | Triple Bottom Line | Stakeholder Theory | Corporate Citizenship | Sustainable Development |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Proponent** | Archie B. Carroll (1991) | John Elkington (1994) | R. E. Freeman (1984) | Logsdon & Wood; others (1990s) | WCED / Brundtland (1987) |
| **Core Focus** | Hierarchical layers of duty | Three parallel performance pillars | Managing relationships with all parties | Firm as a citizen | Inter-generational equity |
| **Structure** | Vertical pyramid | Three interconnected circles | Network / map | Concentric circles | Integrated triangle |
| **Primary Metric** | CSR intensity per layer | 3Ps measured separately | Stakeholder salience score | Citizenship index (LGI/Citizen Index) | SDG progress score |
| **Key Strength** | Clarity of obligation priority | Balanced measurement of all capitals | Inclusivity of all voices | Rights & duties parity | Long-term, holistic view |
| **Key Limitation** | Layering is debated as rigid | Difficult to monetize "Planet" & "People" | Hard to prioritize competing claims | Lacks operational metrics | Abstract, hard to localize |
| **Indian Application** | Section 135, Companies Act 2013 | SEBI BRSR framework | NGRBC (National Guidelines) | Corporate Citizen Charter | India's NDCs & SDG localization |
| **KTU Marker Words** | *"Bottom-up obligation"* | *"3Ps / People-Planet-Profit"* | *"Power-Legitimacy-Urgency"* | *"Right to operate"* | *"Brundtland Definition"* |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation — Carroll's Pyramid Justification

> **Board Examiner's Note:** KTU valuators award marks for the *reasoning* behind the pyramid structure, not just listing the layers. Below is the exact step-by-step logic expected.

**Step 1: Identify the Firm's Existential Purpose.**
A business enterprise is fundamentally a *socio-economic institution* whose primary purpose is to create value through the production of goods and services.

$$
\text{Primary Purpose} = \max(\text{Profit}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \text{Legal Compliance}
$$

**Step 2: Acknowledge Legal Framework.**
Society codifies minimum conduct through laws. Non-compliance threatens the firm's *license to operate*. This justifies the **second layer — Legal**.

$$
\text{Threshold} = L_{\text{min}} \quad \text{where} \quad L_{\text{min}} = \text{Mandatory statutes}
$$

**Step 3: Recognize Ethical Expectations Beyond Law.**
Society expects conduct that is *more than merely legal*. Behaviors like honesty, fairness, and transparency lie above legal compliance.

$$
\text{Ethical Conduct} \supset \text{Legal Compliance} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Ethical Conduct} \neq \text{Legal Compliance}
$$

**Step 4: Aspirational Contribution — Philanthropy.**
At the apex, firms engage in voluntary acts that improve community welfare — but this is *discretionary*, not required for survival.

$$
\text{Philanthropy} = \text{Discretionary Contribution} > 0
$$

**Step 5: Assemble into a Pyramid (Visual Logic).**
- Economic = *foundation* (most firms must meet this).
- Legal = *plateau* (filters firms).
- Ethical = *rising tier* (differentiates good firms).
- Philanthropic = *peak* (distinguishes great firms).

$$
\boxed{\text{CSR}_{\text{Carroll}} = f(E, L, Et, Ph) \quad \text{with} \quad E \geq L \geq Et \geq Ph \text{ in terms of universality}}
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — TBL Mathematical Form

While TBL is qualitative, scholars have attempted **monetization frameworks** to integrate the 3Ps.

**Step 1: Quantify Economic Profit.**
$$
P_{\text{econ}} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Explicit Costs} - \text{Depreciation}
$$

**Step 2: Quantify Social Capital (People).**
Common proxies include:
- Number of beneficiaries
- Hours of employee volunteering
- Investment in training and welfare

$$
S_{\text{social}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot M_i
$$

Where $w_i$ is the weight assigned to social metric $M_i$ (e.g., beneficiaries, volunteer hours).

**Step 3: Quantify Environmental Capital (Planet).**
$$
S_{\text{env}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} v_j \cdot E_j
$$

Where $E_j$ represents environmental metrics such as $\text{tCO}_2e$ (tonnes of CO₂ equivalent), water consumption in litres, or waste in kg.

**Step 4: Combine into the TBL Composite Score.**
$$
\boxed{\text{TBL Score} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{econ}} + \beta \cdot S_{\text{social}} + \gamma \cdot S_{\text{env}}}
$$

Subject to:
$$
\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1, \quad \alpha, \beta, \gamma > 0
$$

**Interpretation:** A firm is "TBL-sustainable" only if all three weighted terms are non-negative and ideally balanced.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Indian vs Global Best Practices

| Dimension | Indian Best Practice | Global Best Practice |
|---|---|---|
| **Pioneering Legacy** | **Tata Group** — Jamsetji Tata (1868) — first to embed philanthropy in business charter | **Cadbury (1824)** — early worker welfare in UK |
| **Education** | **Infosys Foundation** — 10,000+ libraries, mid-day meals | **Microsoft** — *TEALS* (Technology Education and Literacy in Schools) |
| **Agriculture / Rural** | **ITC e-Choupal** — 4M+ farmers, 6,500 kiosks | **Coca-Cola** — *Project Unnati* (mango farming in India) |
| **Environment** | **Mahindra & Mahindra** — *Nanhi Kali* (education) + *Harit Mahindra* (green cover) | **Patagonia** — *1% for the Planet* |
| **Health** | **Tata Trusts** — cancer hospitals, mobile medical units | **Novartis** — access-to-medicines programs in Africa |
| **Disaster Relief** | **Adani Foundation** — COVID-19 response, flood relief | **Google.org** — crisis response grants |
| **Tech for Good** | **Wipro Earthian** — sustainability education in 1,000+ schools | **Google AI for Social Good** |
| **KPIs Tracked** | CSR-2 spend, lives impacted, beneficiaries | ESG scores, SBTi targets, B Corp certification |

## 3.5 The "How" Behind Each Best Practice

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition (KTU 2024):** Top-scoring answers always link a best practice to the *CSR model* it exemplifies. Use the table below as a model-mapping cheat sheet.

| Company / Initiative | Primary CSR Model Illustrated | Strategic Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Tata Group** | Carroll's Pyramid (Philanthropic apex) + Corporate Citizenship | Legacy of trust; license to operate in 100+ countries |
| **Infosys Foundation** | TBL (People + Planet) + Stakeholder | Brand value creation; employee pride; talent retention |
| **ITC e-Choupal** | TBL (Planet + People + Profit) + Stakeholder | Secures supply chain, empowers farmers, reduces carbon |
| **Patagonia** | TBL (Planet-dominant) + Sustainable Development | Authentic brand narrative; customer loyalty |
| **Unilever Sustainable Living Plan** | TBL + Sustainable Development + Stakeholder | *"Decoupling"* growth from environmental impact |
| **Google RE\<C** | TBL (Planet) + Sustainable Development | Renewable energy < cost of fossil fuels |
| **Mahindra Nanhi Kali** | Carroll's Pyramid (Ethical + Philanthropic) | SDG-4 (Quality Education) alignment |

## 3.6 KTU Pattern — How Marks Are Distributed

> [!NOTE]
> For a **14-mark question on CSR Models and Best Practices**, the typical KTU mark distribution is:
> 
> - 3 marks — Definition + Model list
> - 4 marks — Detailed explanation of 1–2 models
> - 4 marks — 2–3 best practice case studies (Indian + Global)
> - 2 marks — Comparative analysis / strategic linkage
> - 1 mark — Conclusion / future relevance
> 
> Always include **at least one Indian + one Global** case study to secure full marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Carroll's CSR Pyramid (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base1["ECONOMIC RESPONSIBILITY - Be Profitable, Viable, Produce Value"]
    base2["LEGAL RESPONSIBILITY - Obey Laws, Pay Taxes, Follow Regulations"]
    base3["ETHICAL RESPONSIBILITY - Be Fair, Honest, Transparent Beyond Law"]
    apex["PHILANTHROPIC RESPONSIBILITY - Contribute to Community Welfare"]
    base1 --> base2 --> base3 --> apex
```

> **Interpretation:** The flow moves from *foundational duties* (base) to *aspirational duties* (apex). The base of the pyramid is widest because economic obligations apply universally; the apex is narrowest because philanthropy is discretionary.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Triple Bottom Line (3Ps Interconnected Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PEOPLE - Social Capital, Human Rights, Community Welfare"] --> C["CORPORATE PERFORMANCE - TBL Score"]
    B["PLANET - Environment, Carbon, Biodiversity, Water"] --> C
    D["PROFIT - Economic Returns, Shareholder Value"] --> C
    A <--> B
    B <--> D
    D <--> A
```

> **Interpretation:** The three pillars are *mutually reinforcing*. A firm cannot sustainably prioritize Profit at the expense of People and Planet — over time, social and environmental degradation erodes economic returns.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Stakeholder Salience Map (Mitchell, Agle & Wood 1997)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["LATENT STAKEHOLDERS"] --> S1["Dormant - Has Power Only"]
    P1 --> S2["Discretionary - Has Legitimacy Only"]
    P1 --> S3["Demanding - Has Urgency Only"]
    P2["EXPECTANT STAKEHOLDERS"] --> S4["Dominant - Power + Legitimacy"]
    P2 --> S5["Dependent - Legitimacy + Urgency"]
    P2 --> S6["Dangerous - Power + Urgency"]
    P3["DEFINITIVE STAKEHOLDERS"] --> S7["Definitive - Power + Legitimacy + Urgency"]
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
```

> **Interpretation:** Stakeholders progress from **Latent** (low salience) to **Expectant** (moderate) to **Definitive** (highest priority). Managers must allocate attention and resources based on the intersection of **Power, Legitimacy, and Urgency**.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Best Practice Categorization (Three Tiers)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T["BEST PRACTICES OF CSR"] --> T1["TIER 1 - STRATEGIC / EMBEDDED"]
    T --> T2["TIER 2 - PROGRAMMATIC / THEMATIC"]
    T --> T3["TIER 3 - PHILANTHROPIC / REACTIVE"]
    T1 --> E1["ITC e-Choupal, Unilever Sustainable Living Plan"]
    T2 --> E2["Infosys Foundation, Google RE_lt_C, Wipro Earthian"]
    T3 --> E3["Tata Trusts, Patagonia 1% for Planet"]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each tier represents a different depth of CSR integration. KTU prefers answers that discuss **Tier 1** (strategic) practices because they demonstrate deeper ethical maturity than mere philanthropy.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Sustainable Development Model (Three-Domain Integration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SD["SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT - Brundtland 1987"] --> EC["ECONOMIC DOMAIN - Growth, Jobs, Trade"]
    SD --> SO["SOCIAL DOMAIN - Equity, Health, Education"]
    SD --> EN["ENVIRONMENTAL DOMAIN - Climate, Biodiversity, Resources"]
    EC <--> SO
    SO <--> EN
    EN <--> EC
    FG["FUTURE GENERATIONS - Inter-generational Equity"] -.-> SD
```

> **Interpretation:** Sustainability is the *intersection* of the three domains, not their union. The dashed arrow to **Future Generations** reflects the Brundtland Report's *inter-generational equity* principle — a KTU favorite definition.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — KTU Answer Architecture for 14-Mark Questions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q["QUESTION - CSR Models and Best Practices"] --> A1["Introduction - Define CSR, Mention Companies Act 2013 Section 135"]
    A1 --> A2["Body Part 1 - Carroll's Pyramid with 4 layers and diagram"]
    A1 --> A3["Body Part 2 - TBL with 3Ps and formula"]
    A1 --> A4["Body Part 3 - Stakeholder Theory with salience"]
    A2 --> A5["Best Practice 1 - TATA - Philanthropic Apex"]
    A3 --> A6["Best Practice 2 - ITC e-Choupal - 3Ps Balanced"]
    A4 --> A7["Best Practice 3 - PATAGONIA - Stakeholder Trust"]
    A5 --> A8["Comparative Analysis - India vs Global, Strategic vs Philanthropic"]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9["Conclusion - TBL + Stakeholder future of CSR, link to SDGs"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This flow doubles as a *writing scaffold* for KTU 14-mark answers. Follow it sequentially — Introduction → Models → Case Studies → Comparison → Conclusion — to hit the **CO1 (Understand), CO2 (Apply), CO4 (Analyze)** outcome mapping of PECBT756.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A consists of *8 to 10 questions* of 3 marks each, to be answered in *one paragraph* (typically 60–80 words). The model answers below are the *exact length* expected by the board examiner.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**List and briefly explain the four layers of Carroll's CSR Pyramid.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991) consists of four hierarchical responsibilities. **(i) Economic** — the foundation; firms must be profitable, produce value, and sustain operations. **(ii) Legal** — firms must obey all applicable laws, pay taxes, and follow regulations. **(iii) Ethical** — firms should conduct business fairly, honestly, and transparently, *beyond* legal compliance. **(iv) Philanthropic** — the apex; firms should voluntarily contribute to community welfare through donations, volunteering, and social programs. Together, the pyramid suggests that **economic and legal obligations are foundational, ethical conduct is expected, and philanthropy is aspirational.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is the Triple Bottom Line? Explain its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, proposed by **John Elkington in 1994**, is a sustainability framework that measures corporate performance across three dimensions: **(i) People (Social)** — fair wages, human rights, community welfare; **(ii) Planet (Environmental)** — carbon footprint, biodiversity, resource conservation; and **(iii) Profit (Economic)** — sustainable financial returns. A firm is considered TBL-compliant only when it creates value across *all three* bottom lines simultaneously, ensuring that economic growth does not come at the cost of social equity or environmental integrity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B offers *internal choice* — students must answer **ONE** out of two 14-mark questions. Both options below are complete, self-contained, and aligned with the **2024 Scheme pattern** (sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail **Carroll's Pyramid of CSR** and the **Triple Bottom Line** model. Compare their focus, structure, and key strengths.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any three best practices of CSR** — at least one Indian and one global — with reference to the models explained in part (a).

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Carroll's Pyramid of CSR (1991) [3 Marks]**

Carroll's Pyramid arranges corporate responsibilities in a hierarchical four-layer structure:
- **Economic Responsibility (Base):** Produce goods/services profitably; ensure operational viability.
- **Legal Responsibility:** Comply with all laws, regulations, and statutes of the land.
- **Ethical Responsibility:** Act with fairness, honesty, and integrity, even where not legally required.
- **Philanthropic Responsibility (Apex):** Voluntarily contribute to community welfare through charitable programs.

**Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994) [2 Marks]**

TBL measures corporate success across three equal pillars: **People (social equity), Planet (environmental stewardship), and Profit (economic prosperity)**. A TBL-compliant firm creates value across all three simultaneously.

**Comparison [2 Marks]**

| Parameter | Carroll's Pyramid | Triple Bottom Line |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Hierarchical (vertical) | Parallel (three pillars) |
| Focus | Priority of obligations | Balanced performance |
| Strength | Clarity of duty order | Holistic measurement |
| Limitation | Debated rigidity of layers | Difficult monetization of 3Ps |

**Conclusion:** While Carroll's model guides *what* a firm owes, TBL guides *how* it measures success.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Best Practice 1 — Tata Group (India) [2.5 Marks]**

Tata Group, led by founder **Jamsetji Tata** in 1868, institutionalized philanthropy through **Tata Trusts**, which own 66% of Tata Sons. Initiatives include **Tata Memorial Hospital** (cancer care), **Tata Institute of Social Sciences**, and rural livelihood programs. This exemplifies the **Philanthropic Apex** of Carroll's Pyramid and the **People + Profit** dimensions of TBL.

**Best Practice 2 — ITC e-Choupal (India) [2 Marks]**

Launched in 2000, **ITC's e-Choupal** digitalizes the agricultural supply chain via 6,500+ kiosks, empowering 4 million+ farmers with real-time pricing, weather data, and quality inputs. It generates a measurable **TBL impact** — *People* (farmer income up 25–40%), *Planet* (reduced transport emissions), and *Profit* (sustained agri-business for ITC).

**Best Practice 3 — Unilever Sustainable Living Plan (Global) [2.5 Marks]**

Launched in 2010, Unilever's plan committed to: (i) improving health & well-being of 1 billion people, (ii) halving environmental footprint, and (iii) enhancing livelihoods of millions. By 2020, Unilever reported reaching 1.3 billion people and a **third-party-verified TBL dashboard**, exemplifying *strategic, embedded* CSR rather than mere philanthropy.

**Concluding Linkage [Implicit — 0 marks separate]:** All three best practices demonstrate that **mature CSR transcends philanthropy** and embeds social/environmental value into the core business model.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Stakeholder Theory** of CSR. Explain Mitchell, Agle & Wood's **Stakeholder Salience Model** with its three attributes and seven stakeholder types.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Identify and elaborate on **two Indian and two global best practices of CSR**, mapping each to the appropriate CSR model.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Stakeholder Theory (Freeman, 1984) [3 Marks]**

R. Edward Freeman's Stakeholder Theory posits that a firm has responsibilities not only to shareholders but to **all parties affected by its operations** — including employees, customers, suppliers, community, government, and the environment. The theory argues that **long-term value creation** depends on managing relationships with all stakeholders, not just maximizing shareholder returns. Stakeholders are classified as **primary** (direct economic relationship) or **secondary** (socio-environmental relationship).

**Mitchell, Agle & Wood's Salience Model (1997) [4 Marks]**

Mitchell, Agle & Wood identified **three stakeholder attributes**:
- **Power** — ability to impose will
- **Legitimacy** — socially accepted claim
- **Urgency** — time-sensitivity of claim

Based on combinations of these attributes, stakeholders fall into **seven types**:

| Type | Power | Legitimacy | Urgency | Managerial Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dormant | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Low — monitor |
| Discretionary | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | Low — ethical duty |
| Demanding | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Annoying — monitor |
| Dominant | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Important — keep satisfied |
| Dependent | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | Need help from powerful allies |
| Dangerous | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Risky — pre-empt |
| **Definitive** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Highest priority** |

**Conclusion:** Managers should allocate time and resources dynamically based on salience, not treat all stakeholders equally.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Indian Best Practice 1 — Infosys Foundation [2 Marks]**

Established in 1996, the **Infosys Foundation** (chairperson: Sudha Murty) supports education, healthcare, rural development, and art. Its initiatives — including **mid-day meals** to 1.5 million+ children, **16,000+ libraries** in government schools, and **disaster relief** during COVID-19 — demonstrate the **Ethical + Philanthropic** layers of Carroll's Pyramid and a strong **People** focus under TBL. The Foundation's *strategic linkage* to Infosys' brand and talent pipeline reflects mature stakeholder management.

**Indian Best Practice 2 — Mahindra & Mahindra — Nanhi Kali [1.5 Marks]**

The **Nanhi Kali** program (since 1996, supported by Mahindra) provides educational support to underprivileged girls. Aligned with **SDG-4 (Quality Education)** and **SDG-5 (Gender Equality)**, it reflects the **Corporate Citizenship model** and Stakeholder Theory's commitment to secondary stakeholders (community).

**Global Best Practice 1 — Patagonia [1.5 Marks]**

Patagonia, the U.S. outdoor apparel company, donates **1% of total sales** to environmental NGOs through *"1% for the Planet"*. Its **"Don't Buy This Jacket"** campaign on Black Friday 2011 exemplified **Sustainable Development** thinking — encouraging reduced consumption. Patagonia is a certified **B Corporation** and exemplifies the **Planet** pillar of TBL.

**Global Best Practice 2 — Google.org — AI for Social Good [2 Marks]**

Google's philanthropic arm, **Google.org**, invests over **USD 1 billion annually** in non-profits and social enterprises. Initiatives like **AI for Social Good** (flood forecasting in India, biodiversity monitoring) reflect **Stakeholder Theory** by addressing *future generations* and *global commons*. Google's **carbon-neutral commitment since 2007** and **RE\<C** renewable energy program align with TBL's Planet pillar and Carroll's Ethical layer.

**Concluding Linkage:** All four practices — Indian and global — demonstrate that **best-in-class CSR is strategic, measurable, and aligned with one or more of the five CSR models** studied in Module 4.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> 
> 1. **🚫 Listing models without explanation:** Writing *"Carroll's Pyramid has 4 layers"* earns *0 marks*. You MUST explain *each* layer with examples (1.5 marks allocated for the elaboration).
> 
> 2. **🚫 Generic best practices:** "Tata does CSR" is worth nothing. You must specify *which initiative*, *when started*, *scale of impact*, and *which CSR model it exemplifies*.
> 
> 3. **🚫 Forgetting the Proponent + Year:** Always attribute models to their authors: *"Carroll (1991)"*, *"Elkington (1994)"*, *"Freeman (1984)"*, *"Brundtland (1987)"*. Missing this costs **½ to 1 mark** per model.
> 
> 4. **🚫 All-Indian / All-Global case studies:** The board specifically rewards *balance*. Always include at least **1 Indian + 1 Global** example.
> 
> 5. **🚫 No diagram:** A hand-drawn pyramid or 3-circles TBL diagram earns **1–2 extra marks** in the valuation. KTU examiners explicitly scan for diagrams in CSR answers.
> 
> 6. **🚫 Confusing Carroll's Layers with Maslow's Hierarchy:** They are NOT the same. Carroll's base is *Economic* (not physiological). Do not interchange.
> 
> 7. **🚫 Treating CSR = Charity:** Philanthropy is only the *apex* of Carroll's Pyramid. KTU rewards answers that distinguish **strategic CSR** from **philanthropic CSR**.

---
